          The estimated cost of this report for the Department of the Navy (DON) is
          $56,902. The estimated total cost for supporting the DON budget justification
OPN 1
          material is approximately $3,113,561 during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes
          $202,708 in supplies and $2,910,853 in labor.
          The estimated cost of this report for the Department of the Navy (DON) is
          $98,398. The estimated total cost for supporting the DON budget justification
OPN 2
          material is approximately $3,113,561 during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes
          $202,708 in supplies and $2,910,853 in labor.
          The estimated cost of this report for the Department of the Navy (DON) is
          $22,577. The estimated total cost for supporting the DON budget justification
OPN 3
          material is approximately $3,113,561 during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes
          $202,708 in supplies and $2,910,853 in labor.
          The estimated cost of this report for the Department of the Navy (DON) is
          $37,623. The estimated total cost for supporting the DON budget justification
OPN 4
          material is approximately $3,113,561 during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes
          $202,708 in supplies and $2,910,853 in labor.
          The estimated cost of this report for the Department of the Navy (DON) is
          $28,617. The estimated total cost for supporting the DON budget justification
OPN 5-8
          material is approximately $3,113,561 during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes
          $202,708 in supplies and $2,910,853 in labor.

              UNCLASSIFIED

                                     Volume 3 - vi
                         Department of Defense Appropriations Act, 2026

Other Procurement, Navy

For procurement, production, and modernization of support equipment and materials not
otherwise provided for, Navy ordnance (except ordnance for new aircraft, new ships, and ships
authorized for conversion); the purchase of passenger motor vehicles for replacement only;
expansion of public and private plants, including the land necessary therefore, and such lands and
interests therein, may be acquired, and construction prosecuted thereon prior to approval of title;
and procurement and installation of equipment, appliances, and machine tools in public and
private plants; reserve plant and Government and contractor-owned equipment layaway,
$17,904,970,000 to remain available for obligation until September 30, 2028, of which $1,076,000
shall be available for the Navy Reserve and the Marine Corps Reserve: Provided, That such funds
are also available for the maintenance, repair, and modernization of ships.


Fiscal Year (FY) 2026 Overseas Operations Costs funding accounted for in the Base budget total
$5,050,000.

 Description:
 Sonobuoys are air launched expendable, electro-mechanical sensors designed to relay underwater sounds associated with ships and submarines to remote processors, critical to U.S. Navy Air Anti-Submarine
 Warfare (ASW) mission to achieve maritime supremacy against near peer threats. Sonobuoys, by type, are procured in quantity based on Fleet demand and to support combat stock levels set by modeling
 conducted as part of the annual Navy's Munitions Requirements Process (NMRP). The Sonobuoy program buys-to-budget based on actual fleet usage, forecasted usage and best negotiated pricing across
 each sonobuoy type. The annual NMRP model estimates inventory requirements for combat stock and all usage requirements for readiness, current operations and squadron training/test. Sonobuoys currently
 support the P-8, P-3 and H-60 platforms.

 [P5 / Sonobuoys, All Types]: In FY24, the sonobuoy industrial base transitioned from a sole source to a competitive environment with multiple vendors. Unit costs are estimated based on a Independent
 Government Cost Estimate (IGCE) that uses historical unit prices adjusted for vendor base standup, learning curves, inflation, establishment of new supply chains germane to independent company designs, and
 economical impact of splitting USG annual procurements across multiple vendors.

 [P5 / AN/SSQ-36 (BT) QZ001]: The AN/SSQ-36 Bathythermograph (BT) is a bathythermograph sonobuoy used to provide a vertical temperature profile of the ocean with respect to depth. The data is transmitted
 to aircraft to assist in the selection of hydrophone depths and tactics for localizing and tracking submarines and long-range forecasts of acoustic conditions in the ocean.

 [P5 / AN/SSQ-53 (DIFAR) QZ002]: The AN/SSQ-53 Directional Frequency Analysis and Recording (DIFAR) is a passive directional sonobuoy which provides acoustic bearing and frequency information used to
 detect, classify, and localize undersea targets of interest.

 [P5 / AN/SSQ-62 (DICASS) QZ004]: The AN/SSQ-62F Directional Command Active Sonobuoy System (DICASS) is a commandable, active acoustic directional sonobuoy that provides target bearing and range
 information.

 [P5 / AN/SSQ-101 (Multi-static Coherent Receiver) QZ006]: The AN/SSQ-101B Air Deployable Active Receiver (ADAR) is a commandable passive acoustic sonobuoy with a horizontal planar array that receives
 reflected energy. It is part of the family of multi-static active sensor systems.

 [P5 / AN/SSQ-125/125A (Multi-static Coherent Source) QZ010]: The AN/SSQ-125/125A Multi-static Active Coherent (MAC) Source is a commandable coherent active search sensor. It is part of the family of
 multi-static active sensor systems.

 [P5 / AN/SSQ-125B (Multi-static Coherent Source)]: The AN/SSQ-125B Multi-static Active Coherent (MAC) Source is a very complex commandable coherent active search sensor with additional classified
 capabilities. It is part of the family of multi-static active sensor systems.

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 The level of sonobuoy procurement funding directly enables the Navy's overall Anti-submarine Warfare (ASW) mission and wartime readiness. The Air ASW program buys-to-budget based on actual fleet usage,
 fleet forecasted usage, and best negotiated pricing across all sonobuoy types. This funding profile does not meet inventory requirements for the entire family of air delivered acoustic sensors. This is the result
 of an increase in actual operational expenditures, budget constraints, and an increase in the Total Munitions Requirement (TMR). The Air ASW community continues to use passive capability combined with an
 increase in the more capable Multi-static Active Coherent (MAC) wide area search Concept of Operations (CONOPS), which uses the AN/SSQ-101 and AN/SSQ-125 family of systems. While the transition to
 MAC requires the procurement of more sophisticated sonobuoys, the resulting wide area search capability is more efficient and effective. The MAC capability provides the foundational Air ASW acoustic search
 capability for the P-8 Poseidon, and increasing the inventory of MAC sonobuoys is required to support the combatant commander's ASW mission execution requirements. This funding profile allows the Navy to
 prioritize and meet current operational forward presence requirements while building fleet commander deployment readiness levels in the ASW mission area.

 The FY2026 request for Sonobuoys - All Types includes $249.908M of discretionary and $80.000M of mandatory (reconciliation) funds for a total of $329.908M. The mandatory (reconciliation) funds procure the
 following additional sonobuoy quantities:
 AN/SSQ-36 QTY 2,300
 AN/SSQ-53 QTY 21,827
 AN/SSQ-62 QTY 9,000
 AN/SSQ-101 QTY 748
 AN/SSQ-125B QTY 519
 Further information for this reconciliation request is provided in Section 20004 (Munitions and Supply Chain) of the Reconciliation Exhibit.

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.327 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.364 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."

 Note: at the discretionary funding level, decreased production deliveries will negatively impact operational readiness and ability to sustain a multi-vendor industrial base.

 NOTES:
 1. In FY 2024, the program transitioned from a sole source environment with ERAPSCO Joint Venture to a competitive environment where previous ERAPSCO partners (Sparton and Ultra-Undersea Sensor
 Systems, Inc (USSI)) will each produce fully independent designs vice partnering with each building only the upper or lower assembly. Sparton, USSI and LM will compete annually for USG orders via a
 competitive multiple award delivery order contract (MADOC). In the event only one vendor is qualified, the USG will use sole source contracts as necessary as bridge efforts to full competition. Future unit
 costs are estimated using an Independent Government Cost Estimate (IGCE) derived from FY24/FY25 proposals and contract awards, new vendor base establishment, sonobuoy re-qualification efforts, future
 production learning curves, supply chain changes, and splitting quantities across multiple vendors. USG independent cost estimate utilizes split awards in order to preserve the health of the industrial base.

Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                      P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 1:                                 4048 / Sonobuoys - All Types
Sonobuoys
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                  Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 Actual split will be dependent on company proposals for each annual delivery order. Additionally, production acceptance costs have increased based on increased effort associated with testing product from
 multiple vendors. Funding also supports product acceptance test infrastructure at various locations and may be used to procure strategic critical parts, material and tooling to enable industry base surge during
 wartime/contingency operations and mitigate supply chain risks. FY24 AN/SSQ-36 sonobuoy order is combined with the FY25 order to achieve greater economies of scale. In FY24, the program restarted the
 baseline AN/SSQ-125 production line while vendors continued to qualify an increasingly complex AN/SSQ-125B independent design. In FY25, the program increased procurement quantities of legacy AN/
 SSQ-125 sonobuoys to replenish and grow USN inventory toward peacetime/wartime objectives and achieve AN/SSQ-101 and AN/SSQ-125 inventory balance as the 2 buoy types are deployed in pairs.

 2. Program buys-to-budget. New MADOC does not have maximum quantity limitations, maximizing industry capacity; ceiling is based on number of available vendors per buoy variant and price points based on
 quantity procured. Program seeks to achieve economies of scale by utilizing both Other Procurement Navy (OPN) and Foreign Military Sales (FMS) funds. Under the MADOC, production quantities will be split
 amongst qualified vendors for the health of the industrial base, reducing economic ordering quantities.

 3. FY 2025 assumes two vendors will be qualified and compete for orders for AN/SSQ-36 and AN/SSQ-53 sonobuoys. Unit costs assume a split between two vendors to ensure health/viability of the industrial
 base, leading to loss of economical ordering quantities, and higher unit prices. FY25 AN/SSQ-62 and legacy AN/SSQ-125 orders will be contracted as sole source as vendors continue to qualify independent
 buoy designs. FY25 quantity and unit cost adjustments are a result of extensive qualification costs, learning curve reset for independent buoy designs and independent production lines. Program plans to defer
 AN/SSQ-101 buoy order to FY26 to better align AN/SSQ-101 and AN/SSQ-125 inventory levels and avoid demil expenditures; FY26 AN/SSQ-101 order will utilize FY24, FY25 and FY26 funds to optimize
 procurement strategy and gain economies of scale.

 4. FY 2026 assumes competitive awards for AN/SSQ-36B and AN/SSQ-53 sonobuoys; MADOC/OTA contract vehicles for AN/SSQ-62 and AN/SSQ-101 sonobuoy awards. As of FY26, AN/SSQ-125 production
 plans to shift solely to AN/SSQ-125B with only one vendor assumed in FY26 with an escalated unit cost between $11K and $15K as a result of vendor provided Rough Order of Magnitude (ROM) received from 2
 vendors.

 OOC:
 FY 2024 includes $10.206M for the OOC budget request.
 FY 2025 includes $28.616M for the OOC budget request.
 FY 2026 includes $5.050M for the OOC budget request to replenish FY23 operational expenditures. Sonobuoy inventory is depleted and falls short of the Total Munitions Requirement adversely affecting
 overseas operations.

 (†)
       indicates the presence of a P-5a

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    As of FY 2024, program transitions from sole source Joint Venture contract with ERAPSCO to a competitive multiple award delivery order contract. Unit costs reflect FY24 negotiated prices and include costs
 associated with establishment of a new vendor base, extensive sonobuoy qualification costs, future production learning curves associated with vendors producing independent sonobuoy designs. FY24 AN/
 SSQ-36 sonobuoy order was combined with the FY25 order to achieve greater economies of scale. Future quantities will be procured to global floor level.
 (2)
    Program buys to budget. FY24 multiple award delivery order contract (MADOC) does not have maximum quantity limitations; ceiling is based on number of available vendors per buoy variant and price points
 based on quantity procured. Program seeks to achieve economies of scale by utilizing both Other Procurement, Navy (OPN) funds and Foreign Military Sales (FMS) funds. FY24 unit costs reflect OTA contract
 awards to allow vendors to continue to qualify independent buoy designs. FY25 marks the onset of a competitive environment whereby unit costs consist of new vendor base and sonobuoy re-qualification costs,
 production learning curves, supply chain demands and vendors splitting production quantities to balance best price and ensure health/viability of industrial base, leading to loss of EOQ (economical ordering
 quantities).
 (3)
    FY24 AN/SSQ-62F unit costs reflect Sole Source OTA contract award to allow single vendor to stand up new production line and ramp up to reliability requirements. Unit costs reflect new vendor base
 establishment, new production line standup, learning curves, supply chain demands. FY25 AN/SSQ-62 MADOC contract to sole source vendor planned late 4Q 2025 for full rate production. FY26 will have 2
 qualified vendors splitting production quantities through MADOC/OTA contract vehicles to ensure industrial base health/viability, potentially resulting in loss of EOQ.
 (4)
    FY24 and FY25 AN/SSQ-101 orders will be combined with FY26 order upon qualification of 2 vendors. MADOC/OTA contract vehicles will be utilized to achieve greater economies of scale and ensure health/
 viability of the industrial base. Actual split will be dependent on company proposals for each annual delivery order.
 (5)
    FY24 AN/SSQ-125 unit cost decrease due to program procuring higher quantities at lower price point from sole vendor while competitive vendor base stabilizes. FY25 order combined with Foreign Military
 Sales (FMS) orders to procure maximum quantities achieving reduced unit costs with economical ordering quantities. AN/SSQ-125 production will shift solely to the more complex and expensive AN/SSQ-125B
 sonobuoy required for additional search detection capability. Program plans to combine additional FY25 program increase funds with FY26 funds for a 1Q FY26 AN/SSQ-125B award to achieve increased
 economies of scale.
 (6)
    As of FY26, AN/SSQ-125 production will shift solely to AN/SSQ-125B with only one vendor assumed in FY26 with an escalated unit cost between $11K and $15K as a result of vendor provided Rough Order
 of Magnitude (ROM) received from 2 vendors. Program plans to procure AN/SSQ-125B order with FY25 and FY26 funding to accelerate inventory goals, achieve economies of scale and better unit cost for this
 very complex multi-static active coherent sonobuoy with additional search detection capability.
 (7)
    Production Engineering and Lot Acceptance Testing costs increased as of FY24 with the onset of multiple vendors producing independent buoy variants to support increased engineering requirements and lot
 acceptance test events required for sonobuoy delivery.

 Description:
 Minotaur Family of Services (MFoS) provides interoperable systems including

 1) Minotaur Core,
 2) Minotaur Data Link Processor (MDLP) (information exchange), and
 3) Data Distribution/Dissemination ((formally Reach/Web) including access to classified National Tactical Means (NTM)/cloud computing services)

 These systems ensure correlation and fusion of tactical mission data products are provided to Combatant Commanders (COCOMs), Fleet Commanders, and Tactical Commanders. MFoS enhances Intelligence,
 Surveillance, Reconnaissance and Targeting (ISR&T) for all warfare pillars in support of the Naval Operational Architecture (NOA). MFoS provides the basis for Battlespace Awareness (BA) tactical decision
 aids, intelligence data distribution, and battle-management coordination with improved decision agility. Funding provides Technical Data Packages supporting ISR&T capabilities, to include Tasking, Collection,
 Processing, Exploitation, and Dissemination (TCPED) airborne, afloat and ashore systems. Funding provides for an increase in ISR&T/TCPED capabilities, reduced operator workload, and deployment of
 a common system infrastructure across the Fleet as directed by the Chief of Naval Operations (CNO). The effort includes procurement and delivery of capabilities that provide Fleet Commanders access to
 NTM, data storage, data analytics, and an ISR&T sensor grid via Beyond Line of Sight Internet Protocol (B/LOS IP) based networks to enable Distributed Maritime Operations (DMO) and Littoral Operations in
 Contested Environments (LOCE).

 Justification:
 Minotaur Family of Services (MFoS) systems enable an increasingly significant Data-as-a-Service (DaaS) role to close critical kill chain gaps within the Naval Operational Architecture (NOA) and bring data to the
 tactical edge in support of Long Range Fires (LRF) and supports the logical basis for alignment to the Combined Joint All-Domain Command and Control (CJADC2) reference architecture.

 FY26 Minotaur OPN funding provides analysis, Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE), integration, modernization, procurement, production, and delivery of MFoS capabilities, production software releases, and
 obsolescence upgrades to pace the threat. Efforts for code modernization necessary to deliver Minotaur for deployment in evolving NOA and networks airborne, afloat and ashore. Funding provides technology
 refresh and obsolescence of high-performance computing systems for fielding of MFoS products.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                               -           7.911           8.906          9.502             -            9.502             -               -         -            -        -              -
Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -        -              -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -        -              -


 Description:
 This budget line item provides the resources to implement the Navy Fleet Training Range (FTR) Instrumentation Program Plan. These FTRs provide the primary means of fleet combat readiness training. The
 plan addresses the following major procurement areas: Electronic Warfare (EW) systems, simulators, Systems Replacement and Modernization (SRAM), and generic systems such as range computer systems,
 simulation, surveillance systems, and Aerial Targets Range Support equipment including Moving Land Targets (MLT) and Target Threat Simulation Program (TTSP) equipment; MLT includes vehicles, vehicle
 components, and Engineering Change Proposals to upgrade hardware; TTSP equipment includes EW & Threat Payload simulations, electronic countermeasures equipment and active emitter augmentation
 equipment, Tactical Combat Training System (TCTS), Large Area Tracking Range (LATR), Undersea Warfare Training Ranges (USWTR), range infrastructure necessary to enable Live Virtual Constructive (LVC)
 training capability, and Link Inject To Live (LITL) provides constructive training information from ground based stations to live aircraft. The integral parts of these major range programs include but are not limited
 to the following: voice communications, weapons scoring systems (Laser Training and Weapons Impact Scoring Sets), display consoles, radars, tracking subsystems, control/ computation subsystems, display/
 debriefing subsystems, processors, HF/ VHF/ UHF receivers, transmitters/ transceivers, multiplexers, intercom circuits, encoding devices, frequency interface control systems, peculiar support equipment, and
 other specialized equipment.

 [P5 / SC004 SRAM - Systems Replacement and Modernization]: The SRAM program provides for the procurement of numerous non-recurring range equipment replacement and modernization efforts that are
 needed at all Naval training ranges. SRAM procurements replace and modernize economically unmaintainable systems and equipment in order to increase range efficiency. Funding for installation of minor
 equipment is required in all years for all ranges. Sample procurements include electronic warfare systems, weapons scoring systems, laser training systems, secure communications equipment, ocean systems
 acoustical equipment, microwave data links, telecom fiber upgrades, video equipment replacement for security risk reduction, and electrical generators.

 [P5 / SC012 OS - Ocean Systems]: Funds the multi-year procurement and upgrade of fixed underwater training ranges at Pacific Missile Range Facility (PMRF), Barking Sands, HI and Southern California Off-
 Shore Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) Range (SOAR), CA. Instrumented undersea ranges provide time, space position information for undersea warfare platforms, weapons and targets in support of Fleet
 Training and Test and Evaluation. Upgrades include cyber security enhancements, increased range accuracy, and splash detection capabilities. The underwater ranges are used to provide individual and unit
 level training for basic ASW skills.

 Large exercises such as Composite Training Unit Exercises (COMTUEX), Fleet Exercises (FLEETEX), and Joint Task Force Exercises (JTFX) are conducted in the vicinity of the fixed underwater training ranges.
 Items procured under this cost element include hydrophones, undersea cable, and shore system electronics. Funded programs include Barking Sands Tactical Underwater Range (BARSTUR)/Barking Sands
 Underwater Range Expansion (BSURE) refurbishment and West Coast Undersea Warfare Training Ranges (WC USWTR) which include SOAR, Tanner Bank (TB) and San Clemente Island (SCI).

1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 3:                                  4204 / Weapons Range Support Equipment
Aircraft Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                   Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P5 / SC158 TCTS Common Ground Subsystem]: The Tactical Combat Training System Inc II (TCTS II) will procure fixed and transportable instrumentation equipment for both shore-based and deployable
 (e.g. temporary range, ship) range applications. TCTS II instrumentation will transmit exercise scenarios, simulate/stimulate exercise participants sensors/weapons with the exercise scenario, track all exercise
 participants and events like weapons engagements, and provide accurate, realistic, and timely feedback. TCTS II is building on technology developed for existing tactical training range systems. TCTS II consists
 of airborne instrumentation called Participant Subsystems (PS) and Ground Subsystems (GS) that include Common Ground Subsystem (CGS) and Shipboard Ground Subsystem (SGS) variants. CGS systems
 are procured in FY2026 for MCAS Yuma and Southern California Offshore Range (SCORE).

 [P5 / SC140 TCTS Remote Range Unit]: SC140 The Tactical Combat Training System Inc II (TCTS II) Remote Range Unit (RRU) provides encrypted range communication capability across large Tactical
 Training Range areas, which are somewhat analogous to cellular telephone towers. Unit costs for RRU include site-specific costs for each Training Range and will vary per year. RRUs will be procured in FY2026
 for MCAS Yuma and Southern California Offshore Range (SCORE) including three (3) RRUs for Strike Group Maritime Open Ocean Range (SGMOOVR), which provides a mobile, shipboard capability to support
 Air Wing Training in any location.

 [P5 / TCTS ENGINEERING CHANGE PROPOSAL]: The Tactical Combat Training System Inc II (TCTS II) Engineering Change Proposal (ECP) funds procurement of critical capability updates, both software and
 hardware updates to the TCTS II system to ensure cybersecurity compliance and connectivity with existing range infrastructure in support of connected Live-Virtual-Constructive (LVC) training requirements.

 [P5 / SC040 TCTS Shipboard Ground Subsystem]: The Shipboard Ground Subsystem (SGS) provides Tactical Combat Training System Inc II (TCTS II) connectivity and real-time exercise control on CVN when
 out of range of fixed range infrastructure. The SGS must be integrated with other shipboard systems. One (1) Shipboard Ground Subsystem is being procured in FY2026 for Strike Group Maritime Open Ocean
 Range (SGMOOVR), which provides a mobile, shipboard capability to support Air Wing Training in any location.

 [P5 / SC105 EW - Threat Presentation]: Threat Presentation includes all the necessary components and elements associated with presenting friendly training event participants with an opposing force (OPFOR)
 operating environment that replicates the expected enemy order of battle. The capability of a range to recreate any Electronic Combat electronic order of battle requires a range to simulate or emulate basic
 elements of Electronic Combat such as search, acquisition and tracking radars, anti-aircraft artillery (AAA) systems, surface-to-air missile (SAM) systems, infrared (IR) systems, jammers, coastal threats, airborne
 simulators, and information warfare / command and control systems. Individual pieces that are procured vary from year to year.

 [P5 / SC151 MLT Hardware]: The Moving Land Target (MLT) Program will provide Moving Land Target vehicles to train aircrews in the demands of Close Air Support (CAS), Time Sensitive Targeting (TST),
 Target Identification, and Forward Air Controller (FAC) procedures within a Network Centric Warfighting force. MLT provides aircrews with threat representative targets that provide infrared reactivity, mobility, and
 realistic radar cross-section targets to meet their combat training and testing needs. The MLT program procures Moving Land Target vehicles, vehicle components, and Engineering Change Proposals to upgrade
 hardware.

 [P5 / SC152 MLT Engineering Change Proposals]: Costs include the procurement of Engineering Change Proposals for critical hardware/software/firmware updates to the Moving Land Targets (MLT).

 [P5 / SC106 TTSP]: The Target Threat Simulation Program (TTSP) replicates various threats dependent upon the objectives of the test and evaluation event(s). TTSP procures the payload equipment required to
 electronically enhance aerial/surface targets to provide threat representative Radio Frequency signatures, specifically the Electronic Attack and Threat Radar Emissions (Active Emitters). TTSP accomplishes this
 through a collection of modules which are integrated into the target in various configurations, providing the ability to simulate the RF environment.

 [P5 / SC711 LVC Range Integration]: Funds Range Live Virtual Constructive (LVC) integration allowing real-time telemetry to be received from new aircraft reaching the fleet. Establish persistent integrated
 training between AEGIS, CVW, Maritime & UAS training assets by enabling LVC capability with live aircraft on Tactical Training Range (TTR) sites. Effort will modify range infrastructure at multiple range sites to
 provide secure network connectivity to provide robust integrated training capability. Allows range communications, networking, and mission brief, display, and debrief capabilities at multiple levels of security.

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $2.434 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $1.669 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."



 The Systems Replacement and Modernization (SRAM) funds fleet priorities for modernization efforts at Naval training ranges in support of fleet training wholeness (FTW). Projects are non-recurring efforts that
 vary from year to year and are prioritized on an annual basis to meet fleet training readiness requirements.

 The ACAT IV Undersea Warfare Training Ranges (USWTR) Program of Record (PoR) contract was awarded 8 July 21 for $410M and continues the effort to modernize and deliver six (6) Training Ranges.
 The in-water sensor system and shore electronics for Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) training ranges at Pacific Missile Range Facility (PMRF), Barking Sands, HI, (e.g. Barking Sands Tactical Underwater
 Range (BARSTUR)/Barking Sands Underwater Range Expansion (BSURE) and Southern California ASW Range (SOAR), CA). The effort will be a time-phased procurement exception to the full funding
 policy. Hardware buys will vary in cost from year to year to meet the Fleet's required date for Initial Operational Capability (IOC) which is slated for BARSTUR/BSURE (3QFY2027) and TB (4QFY2030). Full
 Operational Capability (FOC) is slated for SCI (4QFY2031) and SOAR (4QFY2032). FY2026 procurements include funds for In-Water System (IWS) and Display, Processing and Control System (DPCS) Test,
 Range Installation, and the Post Installation Survey. Additionally, this effort funds various Systems Engineering Technical Reviews required to execute the Undersea Warfare Training Range Increment II and III
 programs.

 Funds in FY2026 will continue Full-Rate Production of Tactical Combat Training System Increment II (TCTS II) and continues efforts to field additional participant systems, ground subsystems and remote range
 units to upgrade Southwest Tactical Training Ranges at MCAS Yuma and the Southern California Offshore Range (SCORE). The significant budget decrease for TCTS II starting in FY2026 is eliminating funding
 associated with the deleted requirement for procuring ship-based Internal Mount (IM) participant subsystems. This change was approved via Fleet Instrumentation Plan V14. FY2026 funds the procurement of
 two (2) Common Ground Subsystems (CGS), twelve (12) Remote Range Units (RRU), and one (1) Strike Group Maritime Open Ocean Range (SGMOOVR) SGS to provide encryption capability for Fleet training
 exercises.

 SC105 EW Electronic Warfare (EW) Threat Presentation funding fluctuates from year to year reflecting Navy prioritization of various EW Threat Presentation devices for use at multiple Tactical Training Range
 (TTR) sites, including Fallon, NV; Yuma, AZ; Southern California Tactical Training Range (SCTTR), CA; Townsend, SC; Mid-Atlantic Electronic Warfare Range (MAEWR), NC. FY2026 Fleet Training Wholeness
 Initiative procures additional Electronic Attack pods, EW 5th GEN Modular Remote Threat Emitter Systems, and completes specific number of electronic warfare system technology refreshes based on available
 budget to sustain and improve availability and maintainability of secure authority to operate (ATO) cyber certifications for initial ATO's at Fleet range sites that include EW Opposition Force (OPFOR) training
 capabilities and connect those systems to the Navy Enterprise Tactical Training Network (NETTN).

Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 Funds in FY2026 for Live-Virtual-Constructive (LVC) will provide equipment for additional distributed mission training capability to East Coast and West Coast Tactical Training Ranges (TTR), with voice
 communication and datalink equipment in support of Live Virtual Constructive (LVC) capabilities. Install distributed network capabilities at various West Coast TTR's, according to Fleet priorities, in support of LVC
 capabilities.

 Footnotes:
 (1)
       SC004 SRAM: Increase of $0.160M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to inflation.
 (2)
    SC012 Ocean Systems: Increase of $6.841M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to Ocean Sensor Subsystem (OSS) and Shore Electronic Subsystem (SES), Digital, Processing, and Control Subsystem (DPCS)
 Testing, Installation, and Post Installation Survey for the BARSTUR and BSURE ranges under the time-phased procurement contract. Additionally, FY2026 includes funds for the hardware procurement and
 production efforts for the OSS, SES, DPCS for Tanner Bank and San Clemente Island ranges.
 (3)
   SC158 TCTS Common Ground Subsystem: FY2026 funds the procurement of two (2) complete Common Ground Subsystems (CGS) to provide encryption capability for Fleet training exercises at multiple
 Tactical Training Ranges.
 (4)
    SC140 TCTS Remote Range Unit: FY2026 funds the procurement of twelve (12) Remote Range Units (RRU) to provide encryption capability for Fleet training exercises which includes three (3) RRUs for the
 Strike Group Maritime Open Ocean Range (SGMOOVR). Unit cost increase of $37K for RRUs include site-specific costs associated with range integration and will vary from range-to-range due to site specific
 requirements and year-to-year based on site fielding plans.
 (5)
       TCTS Internal Mount: Decrease from FY2025 to FY2026 due to removal of TCTS II Internal Mount requirement.
 (6)
    The Tactical Combat Training System Inc II (TCTS II) Engineering Change Proposal (ECP): Funding supports the procurement of critical software and hardware updates to TCTS ground subsystems to ensure
 cybersecurity compliance and connectivity with existing range infrastructure in support of connected Live-Virtual-Constructive (LVC) training requirements. Increase of $0.260M from FY2025 to FY2026 is due to
 need for obsolescence ECPs in FY2026.
 (7)
    SC040 TCTS Shipboard Ground Subsystem: Strike Group Maritime Open Ocean Range (SGMOOVR) funding for one (1) Shipboard Ground Subsystem (SGS) unit in FY2026. Increased unit cost of $60.8K
 from FY2025 to FY2026 due to site-specific costs associated with range integration and will vary from range-to-range due to site specific requirements and year-to-year based on site fielding plans.

ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready) :                                                                   MDAP/MAIS Code:
 (8)
   SC105 EW-Threat Presentation: Increase from FY2025 to FY2026 in the amount of $3.219M due to Fleet Training Wholeness Initiative procurement of additional Electronic Attack pods, EW 5th GEN Modular
 Remote Threat Emitter Systems, and Electronic Warfare system technology refreshes to sustain and improve availability and maintainability of secure authority to operate (ATO) cyber certifications for initial
 ATO's at Fleet range sites that include EW Opposition Force (OPFOR) training capabilities and connect those systems to the Navy Enterprise Tactical Training Network (NETTN).
 (9)
    SC151 MLT Hardware: MLT Hardware includes Moving Land Target Vehicles, Ground Control Stations, Command and Control units, Data Links, Radio kits, Range equipment, Relays and other hardware
 such as kits to include weapon instrumentation kits, specialty tires / wheels, and equipment protection. Equipment being procured is based on the operational demand / training requirements of the fleet, which
 vary from year to year; consequently, the items, quantities of items, and associated unit costs of the items being procured, vary from year to year. Hardware procured is not one for one with target vehicles.
 Increase of funds from FY2025 to FY2026 is due to obsolescence-related parts in FY2026.
 (10)
    SC152 MLT ECP: Costs include the procurement of Engineering Change Proposals for critical hardware/software/firmware updates (i.e.: Information Assurance / Windows / System Change Requests
 (SCRs)), as well as engineering system level changes to meet the requirements of the Risk Management Framework (RMF) Information Assurance (IA) processes and all safety/modernization related issues.
 (11)
     SC 106 - TTSP: Hardware quantities and unit costs are dependent upon which threat simulation equipment is being procured; some equipment is more expensive than others and the type of equipment being
 procured is dependent upon Fleet requirements and consumption. It is critical to maintain adequate inventory levels as these assets are required to establish readiness capability for the Fleet. Increase of funds
 from FY2025 to FY2026 is due to obsolescence-related parts in FY2026.
 (12)
     SC711 LVC Range Integration: Decrease of $7.829M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to zeroization of hardware buys for MQ-8 simulators. FY2026 funds provide equipment for initial distributed capability and
 additional capability at East Coast and West Coast Tactical Training Range (TTR), with voice communication and datalink equipment in support of Live Virtual Constructive (LVC) capabilities. Install distributed
 network capabilities at various West Coast TTR's, according to Fleet priorities, in support of LVC capabilities.
 (13)
        SC820 SRAM: Increase of $0.014M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to inflation.
 (14)
    SC820 Ocean Systems: Increase of $0.147M from FY2025 to FY2026 in order to support hardware procurement and production efforts for the Ocean Sensor Subsystem (OSS), Shore Electronic Subsystem
 (SES), and Digital, Processing, and Control Subsystem (DPCS) for Tanner Bank and San Clemente Island ranges.
 (15)
        SC820 TCTS: Decrease of $0.433M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to removal of TCTS II Internal Mount requirement.
 (16)
        SC820 EW: Increase of $0.042M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to inflation.
 (17)
        SC820 LVC Range Integration: Decrease of $0.367M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to removal of MQ-8 requirement.
 (18)
        SC831 SRAM: Increase of $0.040M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to inflation.
 (19)
    SC831 Ocean Systems: Increase in the amount of $0.219M from FY2025 to FY2026 includes Production Engineering support for the hardware procurement and production efforts for the OSS, SES, and
 DPCS for Tanner Bank and San Clemente Island ranges.
 (20)
        SC831 TCTS: Reduction of $1.224M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to removal of TCTS Internal Mount requirement.
 (21)
        SC831 EW: Increase of $0.037M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to inflation.
 (22)
        SC831 LVC Range Integration: Decrease of $0.015M from FY2025 to FY2026 due to removal of MQ-8 requirement.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -         -                -


 Description:
 AVIATION SUPPORT EQUIPMENT provides funds for the procurement of air-launched anti-submarine (ASW) detection and other general support equipment associated with aircraft systems. Other support
 equipment includes ground electronics equipment, aircraft launch and recovery equipment, photographic equipment, reconnaissance and electronic warfare process.
 The items contained within this budget line item were previously funded under the following line items in FY14 and prior:
 4208 Expeditionary Airfields
 4214 Aircraft Rearming Equipment
 4216 Aircraft Launch and Recovery Equipment
 4255 LAMPS MK III Shipboard Equipment

 [P5 / Expeditionary Airfields]: This program provides Expeditionary Airfield Surfacing Equipment for procurement of aircraft recovery equipment, Expeditionary Airfield Surfacing Equipment and accessories,
 Airfield Lighting Equipment and Visual Landing Aids for Naval Aviation Expeditionary Airfields (EAF.) EAF recovery equipment consists of the Marine Corps Expeditionary Arresting Gear and its accessories.
 This equipment is used to stop aircraft in less than 1000 ft. on EAF surfacing equipment and accessories are used to construct airfields of varying configurations such as, 6000+ ft conventional airport runways
 and taxiways, Forward Arming and Refueling Points (FARPs), Forward Operating Bases (FOBs), Landing Zones (LZs), Vertical Takeoff and Landing (VTOL) areas, Vertical Short Takeoff and Landing (VSTOL)
 runways, and parking areas. EAF Lighting equipment augments the many types of EAFs with lighting of the runways, taxiways, parking ramps, LZs, FARPs, FOBs, VTOL pads and VSTOL's, In addition, EAF
 Lighting identifies airspace obstructions and provides wind direction guidance along with pilot controlled capability. Much of the EAF Lighting utilizes Infrared Lighting for use with Night Vision Devices for night
 operations by all Type/Model/Series aircraft. Precision Approach Path Indicator (PAPI) and Omni Directional Approach Lighting Systems (ODALS) are used to guide aircraft to the proper landing or arresting
 gear area of the EAF. This core funding level directly supports the procurement and fielding of operational EAF systems for three Active Marine Aircraft Wings (MAW) and one Reserve MAW, testing and training
 installations, and provides assets for use by the Marine Expeditionary Forces during contingency operations. These systems directly support the Marine Corps ability to be trained and equipped as a naval
 expeditionary force-in-readiness prepared to operate in a contested environment. FY2024 also includes additional funding for replacement of surfacing and lighting equipment lost in support of Over The Horizon
 (OTH) efforts for US Central Command requirement for Expeditionary Airfield Systems aboard Prince Sultan Air Base, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, in support of rotor, tilt rotor, and fixed wing aircraft operations.
 This surfacing and lighting equipment are an EAF War Reserve asset and directly affects the deployment of future crisis support options. Resupply of contingency surfacing and lighting equipment to support the
 Fleet Marine Forces' deployment goals enables commanders the unique capabilities to provide Forward Arming and Refueling Points (FARPs) and Forward Operating Bases (FOBS) in support of unmanned,
 rotary and fixed wing aircraft, enabling performance of a variety of functions to include: medivac, assault support, air reconnaissance, anti-air warfare, offensive air support, and electronic warfare.



LI 4213 - Aircraft Support Equipment                                                                    UNCLASSIFIED

1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 3:                                   4213 / Aircraft Support Equipment
Aircraft Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): B                        Program Elements for Code B Items: 0204112N                               Other Related Program Elements: 0604512N, 0604112N,
                                                                                                                                                   0604530N
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P5 - 2 / Acft Rearming Equip]: This program funds the procurement of common Armament Support Equipment (ASE), and Weapons Support Equipment (WSE) under the procurement and inventory control of
 the Naval Inventory Control Point and the Naval Air Systems Command. This budget line supports: (a) initial outfitting for all in-production weapons systems; (b) procurement of new Support Equipment, and
 (c) procurement of replacement items for obsolete Support Equipment. These items support sustained operations and surge deployments of the CV battle groups. Shipboard/Shorebased WSE is utilized by
 weapons departments to handle, transport, and maintain weapons. Shipboard/Shorebased ASE is utilized by squadrons and supporting activities to load and service aircraft weapons and guns.

 [P5 - 3 / Air Launch & Recovery Equip]: This program provides for the procurement of aircraft launch, recovery, visual landing aids, and related information systems as well as ancillary items required for
 installation aboard aircraft carriers, air capable combatant vessels, amphibious assault ships and shore stations.

 Baseline funding is required for support of 55 ALRE equipment systems. Major categories of ALRE systems support include: Launcher, Recovery, Information Systems, Visual Landing Aides (VLA), and Aviation
 Land and Launch Enclave (ALLE), Aviation Data Management and Control System (ADMACS), Marine Aviation Data Management System - Consolidated Controls (MADMS-CC) and Carrier Aviation Data
 Management System (CADMS).

 This program funds multiple ECPs to rectify critical obsolescence items, resolve evolving Cybersecurity upgrades to correct deficiencies identified through the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA)
 mandated risk assessment process, and to implement technical refresh of critical out of production ALRE systems. ALRE is unique in nature in that systems are very seldom sun-downed. Several current Visual
 Landing Aid (VLA), Launcher and Recovery systems require significant investment due to obsolete 1960's technology.

 The program is working modernization and optimization efforts of Aircraft Launch and Recovery Equipment (ALRE) across all L-Class Amphibious Assault Ship (AAS) platforms and Marine Corps land based
 training facilities that directly support Marine Aviation (MA) per the Commandants Planning Guidance 38 (CPG-38) and Force Design 2030. The ALRE systems onboard these vessels are antiquated, non-
 supportable, and require modernization and optimization with benefits of 21st century technology. The program will divest legacy capabilities, which do not meet future requirements. Modernization plans include
 Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) systems, data automation, and enhanced situational awareness capabilities utilizing industry partners providing technology for system integration and greater cyber resiliency.
 The modernization focus areas will enable CPG 38 and Force Design 2030 execution ensuring USMC advantages over our peer competitors.

 ALRE procurements are initiated due to a variety of reasons including fleet-generated reports associated with safe and reliable operations of existing equipment, support of fixed and rotary wing aircraft on Air
 Capable Ships, and maintaining reliability, availability and maintainability of ALRE equipment. Engineering Change Proposals (ECPs) are generated and processed via a Configuration Control Board. Once
 approved, the ECP final product is a service change kit. These kits are identified for installation aboard applicable ships as well as shore-based installations in the ECP. Major shipboard equipment items are
 generally installed by shipyard personnel, alteration installation teams or fleet readiness centers voyage repair teams during routine or restricted availabilities of the various ships. Service change kits support
 corrective actions that result from changes in operational conditions, obsolescence, and improvements in reliability, availability and maintainability.

 [P5 - 4 / Natural Disaster Recovery]: Severe earthquakes in the Ridgecrest, CA area impacted Naval Air Warfare Station (NAWS) China Lake on 4 July (magnitude 6.4) and 5 July (magnitude 7.1) 2019. As
 a result, NAWS experienced severe damage to equipment within buildings, magazines, airfield pavements, other structures, and infrastructure. Numerous facilities were either partially damaged or rendered
 uninhabitable. Restoration efforts identified buildings, structures, and equipment that either require repair or complete replacement. Funding is required to outfit, update, recalibrate, and replace peculiar support
 equipment needed to restore mission capability.

 [P3A / LAMPS MK III - SRQ(KU)-4 (S1010)]: P3A - 4 / LAMPS MK III - SRQ(KU)-4 (S1010)]: LAMPS MK III, AN/SRQ-4 (Ku), is an over the horizon information communications system with a high-speed, bi-
 directional, digital data link that transmits data from MH-60 helicopters to surface ships (cruisers and destroyers) to provide data, imagery, acoustic, electronic support measures, communications, and radar
 information to the warfighter via the Ku-band link.

 [P3A - 2 / CMV22]: CMV-22 - CMV-22 is replacing the C-2 as the Carrier On-board Delivery (COD) platform. Funding provides for the modification of CVN ships in support of approved CMV-22 Ship Change
 Documents (SCD). These modifications will allow for fire suppressant compliance, blade storage, and space modification necessary to support the CMV-22 mission.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $1.035 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $1.880 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."

 Expeditionary Airfields (EAF) -FY 2026 funding is required for the procurement of EAF Surfacing Equipment, Lighting Equipment, Arresting Gear and associated support costs.

 Aircraft Rearming Equipment - FY 2026 baseline funding is required for A/F32K-1A Weapons Assembly Table Replacement, MHU-126/202 Trailer Replacement (MHU-230/M), FARP Ordnance Loading SE,
 HLU-288/E/HLU-196 Bomb Hoist Replacement, and associated support costs.

 Aircraft Launch and Recovery Equipment (ALRE) -

 Information Systems: FY 2026 funding required to complete installs for the modification of the Wind Processor Unit for CVN Shock Requirements Moriah ECP 0325 and associated support. Wind ECP upgrade
 will allow Commanding Officer to make immediate decisions of aircraft sortie generation due to wind speed and direction affecting mission deployment or change to air plans or ship coordinates to allow mission
 operations to keep schedule and ensure lethality planned by the squadrons.

 Information Systems - MA: FY 2026 Marine Aviation funding is required for installation costs of hardware for Moriah Wind Systems and associated support costs. The ALRE Amphibious Assault Ship (AAS)
 Information Systems provides modernization of state of the art wind measuring equipment, boundary defense capability and resiliency providing cyber secure operationally available aviation networks. The
 Moriah Wind System and aviation enclave allow data sharing with external systems including Joint Precision Approach and Landing Systems (JPALS) and Navigation Sensor System Interface (NAVSSI) for
 greater situational awareness in a cyber-secure environment. The modernization of these systems allow more sustainable equipment providing the Combatant Commander and Commanding Officers mission
 critical data to allow more effective naval expeditionary ARG-MEU operations.



LI 4213 - Aircraft Support Equipment                                                                            UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           Volume 3 - 51

Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                         Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                      P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 3:                                 4213 / Aircraft Support Equipment
Aircraft Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): B                       Program Elements for Code B Items: 0204112N                             Other Related Program Elements: 0604512N, 0604112N,
                                                                                                                                                0604530N
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 Visual Landing Aids: FY 2026 funding is required for additional kit procurements, installs and associated support costs for the following ECPs: Digital Integrated Launch and Recovery Television Surveillance
 System (Digital ILARTS), Land-based Optical Landing Aid (LOLA), 1000W Floodlight Conversion to LED and Horizon Reference Set (HRS) Tech Refresh ECP. The program will also continue the non-recurring
 engineering for IFLOLS Phase 5 ECP. ILARTS must be updated from an antiquated analog system to a digital system to address numerous equipment obsolescence issues. Analog equipment is no longer
 available for procurement and if there is no transition to digital data, then CVNs will no longer be able to survey, record, and distribute video of flight deck and surrounding airspace. If LOLA equipment is not
 procured, then land based sites will not have shipboard equivalent optical landing aids for pilot training. LOLA has been a top priority request from the Landing Signal Operator (LSO) school for the past ten years.

 Visual Landing Aids - MA: FY 2026 VLA Marine Aviation funding is required for kit procurements, installation and associated support costs for Night Vision Device (NVD) Fixture Upgrade, Advanced Stabilized
 Glide Slope Indicator (ASGSI) Basic Angle Range change, Aviation Lighting Control Panel Set (ALS-CPS), and Vertical Short Take Off and Landing Optical Landing System (VSTOL-OLS). The ALRE AAS
 Visual Landing Aids provide modernization and optimization of lighting systems onboard ship and land-based training facilities. These provide Marine Aviation flight operations to be compatible with Night
 Vision Devices (NVD), F-35B Aircraft allowing increased OPTEMPO and squadron size aboard LHA/D ships mapping to Force Design 2030 for USMC lightning carrier CONOPS focused on but not limited to
 USINDOPACOM Theater. It will allow for more sustainable equipment, additional F-35B night landing zones (SPOT 9), and pilot compatibility which will allow for more lethal ARG-MEU and Expeditionary Strike
 Group operations.

 Visual Landing Aids N95: OPNAV N95 FY 2026 funding is for the procurement of LHA/D Spot 9 kits and associated support costs. The Spot 9 Lighting Upgrade is required to increase existing LHA/D flight deck
 marking and lighting to provide a second day/night certified F-35B recovery spot to meet requirements cited within the LHA(R) Flight Zero and Flight One AAS Replacement CDD. Spot 9 improves the safety
 flexibility during night operations by allowing increased ops tempo and enables USN/USMC 'Lighting Carrier' conops vision to be achieved. A second day/night certified F-35B recovery spot will also mitigate
 operational safety concerns if Spot 7 becomes unavailable due to an aircraft incident or loss of visual landing aids.

 Launcher: FY 2026 funding is required for continuation of ECP-I-CAT-0504 Digital Clock Timer Relay Replacement, ECP-I-CAT-0517 Improved Nose Gear Launch Deck Tensioner Design, ECP-I-CAT-0535
 Launch Valve Cut Out Switch, ECP-I-CAT-0582 Improved Digital End Speed Indicator (IDESI), ECP-I-JBD-0377 JSF Jet Blast Deflectors, and ECP-I-CAT-0456 Single Purpose Controller (Autolube Multi-Purpose
 Controller Replacement).

 Recovery: FY 2026 funding is required for the continuation of ECP-I-ARC-0455 ARC Long Term Display, ECP-I-AG-0540 MEC Packing, ECP-I-AG-0469 Accumulator Floating Piston, Wire Supports Material
 Replacement, Recovery Assist, Secure and Traverse (RAST) Electronic Component Assembly (ECA), RAST Winch Hydraulic Power Unit (WHPU), and Compact Swaging Machine.

 ADMACS: FY 2026 funding required for ADMACS ECP-0578 Switch Firewall and Rack redesign (FORD Class only) and ECP-TBD for Windows and Java Appletts (ATO driver).

 MADMS - MA: FY 2026 funding required to continue non-recurring engineering, procure kits and associated support costs for the Marine Aviation Data Management System - Consolidated Controls (MADMS-
 CC) program. MADMS-CC is the ADMACS Marine Aviation L-Type variant. MADMS-CC provides ALRE modernization of air and flight operations supporting work centers on Amphibious Assault Ships (AAS).
 Capabilities will respond to the time critical operational need to deploy, employ, sustain, and redeploy aviation assets meeting a cyber-resilient expeditionary environment. Development of MADMS-CC on AAS
 will support integration of F-35Bs into the ARG/MEU. This system will enable increased F-35B OPTEMPO that maps to CPG-38 and Force Design 2030 enabling USMC lightning carrier CONOPS focused
 on, but not limited to, USINDOPACOM Theater. This modernized MADMS-CC equipment will permit the Expeditionary Strike Groups (ESG) to communicate with greater efficiency enabling greater situational
 awareness across the battlefield, accelerated operational decisions and enhancing command and control.

 Remarks:
 The quantities of Expeditionary Airfield Surfacing Equipment procured vary depending on the type of matting and service change requirements each year. The equipment, accessories, and service changes
 are procured and fielded with these funds. Equipment procurements are based on inventory shortfalls, product improvements to fill or correct deficiencies, modernizing EAF equipment to improve availability,
 maintainability, reliability, supportability, deploy-ability, safety-of-flight, and to keep pace with new aircraft and aircraft systems. EAF's modernization will focus special emphasis on capabilities across the full
 spectrum of expeditionary military operations, focusing on cross theater deploy-ability, inter-theater mobility, and enabling the community to support day-to-day consequence of being the force-in-readiness.
 The return on investment of updated equipment will provide commanders the flexibility required to support the ever changing flow on the battlefield. The continual adaptation of new technologies will ensure this
 program maintains pace with the rest of the world.

 (†)
       indicates the presence of a P-5a

 Footnotes:
 (1)
   AM-2 Matting (F72) (1.1.1) - The increase in FY26 is to account for inflation of unit cost for Lightweight Matting equipment specifically designed for Vertical Takeoff and Landing (VTOL) and Vertical and Short
 Takeoff and Landing (VSTOL) missions which will serve as a compliment to existing AM2 until a lightweight matting can be developed.
 (2)
    Lightweight Matting (1.1.5) procures updated surfacing components and materiel that enable the war fighter to install and support VTOL, VSTOL, Taxiway, and Parking surfaces faster than ever before, while
 requiring a fraction of the support equipment, and logistical burden currently required. This ECP of current legacy surfacing systems including light-duty mat and medium-duty mat focused on providing more
 mobile surfacing systems, to modernize EAF equipment and improve availability, maintainability, reliability, supportability and deployability of these systems while continuing to maintain interoperability with AM2.
 (3)
    Surfacing Engineering Change Proposals (1.1.6) supports the procurement of hardware to support multiple Surfacing ECPs and the associated engineering support. Surfacing ECPs are required to adapt
 to diverse airfield operating environments which drive changes to tool kits necessary for matting installation, accommodating new aircraft heat signatures (i.e. JSF, V22) as well as variable soil compositions.

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     Volume 3 - 55

                                                                                            UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-5, Cost Analysis: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                                      Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                         P-1 Line Item Number / Title:                                                  Item Number / Title [DODIC]:
1810N / 03 / 3                                                                 4213 / Aircraft Support Equipment                                              1 / Expeditionary Airfields
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready) :                                                                  MDAP/MAIS Code:
 Increase in this line in FY 2026 directly supports the CPG in the INDO-PACOM Theater as changes in mission direction require ECP of current legacy surfacing equipment focused on providing mobile surfacing
 systems. This will modernize EAF surfacing equipment and improve availability, maintainability, reliability, supportability and deploy-ability.
 (4)
    Surfacing Equipment Acceptance Test & Evaluation (2.1.1) Address the Acceptance Testing required to support multiple Surfacing ECPs. Efforts will increase in FY2026 - in support of new Expeditionary
 Airfield Surfacing capability, specifically, the Anchoring Optimization, Dual Cone Penetrometer Replacement, Rut Analysis using Infrared Detector, and Rockdrill.
 (5)
    Lighting Engineering Change Proposals (3.1.2) address the obsolescence and reliability of the currently utilized aging legacy system as well as the CAT I Instrument Flight Rules and Night Vision Device. FY
 2026 funds will be used to improve and procure Expeditionary Air Fields; Approach lighting, Runway lighting, and commercial Airfield lighting solution hardware, including upgrading commercial ECP solutions.
 Lighting ECPs will improve readiness and safety to ensure Navy/Marine Corps aircraft can land at night in austere environments. EAF has been directed per HQMC to increase airfield light capability to overcome
 aging systems and meet updated forward deployment table of basic allowance requirements. COTS LED Airfield Upgrade ECP will provide a more deployable, maneuverable modernized commercial based
 system that meets the above requirements while allowing procurement at an expeditious rate in-line with the CPG and ASN RDA direction.

 Footnotes:
 (6)
    Updated estimate from contractor includes step-ladder pricing with cost per unit of $34-$37K based on quantity purchased through last ordering period in FY 2026. New Contract to be awarded in FY 2027 with
 estimated unit cost of $38K. Accelerated MHU-230 Procurement in FY 2024 to address increasing obsolescence issues with legacy trailers.
 (7)
    SPOT will be used to support USMC Forward Rearming and Refueling Point (FARP) operations capable of supporting both fixed and rotary wing aircraft in remote, undeveloped locations. Four Pilot
 Production Units (PPUs) will be ordered in FY 2025. Production will begin in FY 2026. USMC Conops require accelerated fielding of this capability, negating the testing and evaluation originally planned in FY
 2026.
 (8)
    Bomb Hoist Replacement will support both Navy and USMC to load/download stores from aircraft wing stations. Purchase of Four Pilot Production Units (PPUs) were moved from FY24 to FY25 due to
 additional engineering and development time required to finalize design to ensure end user input and requirements were incorporated. Four PPUs will be ordered and coupled with mission effectiveness/
 suitability tests and Fleet Evaluation in FY25. Production units to be ordered in FY26.

 Note: Due to space limitations, quantities in the Exhibit P-21 delivery calendar are truncated and rounded based on the maximum quantity in the calendar as follows. If the maximum quantity is less than or equal to than 9,999, all quantities
 are shown as each. If the maximum quantity is between 10,000 and 999,999 all quantities are shown in thousands. If the maximum quantity is between 1,000,000 and 999,999,999 all quantities are shown in millions (rounded to the nearest
 thousand).If the maximum quantity is equal or greater than 1,000,000,000 all quantities are shown in billions (rounded to the nearest million).

LI 4213 - Aircraft Support Equipment                                                                                    UNCLASSIFIED

Exhibit P-5, Cost Analysis: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                                              Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                             P-1 Line Item Number / Title:                                                      Item Number / Title [DODIC]:
1810N / 03 / 3                                                                     4213 / Aircraft Support Equipment                                                  3 / Air Launch & Recovery Equip
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready) :                                                                       MDAP/MAIS Code:
 Remarks:
 [Hardware/Recurring] The recurring hardware funding shown is a combination of many planned hardware upgrades to a variety of Aircraft Launch and Recovery Systems and to a variety of ships/classes
 including Carrier Vessel
 Nuclear (CVNs), Amphibious Assault Ships (AAS), and Air Capable Ships (ACS), which include various unit costs/quantities for differing fiscal years. As such, hardware recurring costs will vary significantly year
 to year and within each item.

 [Hardware/Non-Recurring] Non-Recurring Engineering costs are associated with design, development, systems test and evaluation, tooling, logistics, systems engineering and project management of hardware
 or software Engineering Changes.

 [Support Cost] Integrated Logistics Support costs are associated with the non-level of effort (organic) labor at NAWCAD Lakehurst directly supporting the manufacture of hardware, assembly kits and installation
 kits for many of the service changes.

 [Support Cost] Installation Support:
 1. Installations are subject to change with ship availability schedules.
 2. The year-to-year unit installations costs will vary and can exceed the projected yearly inflation rate due to the hardware installation cost being dependent on quantity and location of the ship (i.e. Norfolk,
 Japan), ship class (DDG, ACS, AAS, CVN), and the ships baseline configuration.
 3. Production lead time includes pre-installation checkout, kitting with other installation hardware and drawings, and time to ship the hardware to the installation site.

 (34)
     C.E. 7.3 Information Systems MA FMP Install: Decrease in FY26 due to completed LPD/LCC install for Moriah Wind Systems (MWS). MWS hardware supporting LPD/LCC was procured to support initial ship
 installation dates provided by the Navy. As operational mission requirements evolved and ship availability schedules changed, the program is still required to install the procured hardware on the new current ship
 availability schedules as required/noted across the FY25 - 27 budget. This will ensure this capability to the Commanding Officers to continue decisive operational ARG/MEU mission execution.
 (35)
        C.E. 7.4 VLA MA FMP Install: FY26 funding required for NVD Fixture Upgrade, ASGSI Basic Angle Range Change and Red Sector Change, and ASGIS Homing Software Update.
 (36)
        C.E. 7.5 Launcher FMP Install: FY26 funding required to complete the JSF Jet Blast Deflector (JBD).
 (37)
        C.E. 7.6 Recovery FMP Install: FY26 funding to complete ARC Long Term Display installations.
 (38)
      C.E. 7.7 ADMACS FMP Install: System Release Upgrade installs occur once a year for 3 or more CVNs. System Release Upgrades addresses software, cybersecurity, and obsolescence, and installs
 on CVNs available when an update is complete and ready for release. Unit cost varies from ship to ship and across years due to the individual ship and length of ship availability, since ADMACS software
 generated is unique to an individual ship to accommodate ship specific spaces (i.e. magazines are mapped to an individual ship's layout). Install schedule is determined by the timeframe constraints of each
 ship's availability, plus the unique modification of software for those ships, and coordination of installs for multiple ships available in that year. The time from procurement to installation varies due to: 1) constantly
 shifting CVN availabilities, and 2) the need to bundle procurements of several kits and do obsolescence upgrade ECPs for each bundle. ADMACS installs require at least a 6 month ship availability.
 (39)
        C.E. 7.9 Visual Landing Aids N95 FMP Install: FY26 funding provided for the LHA/D Spot 9 Lighting Upgrade.

Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                      P-1 Line Item Number / Title:                                                       Modification Number / Title:
1810N / 03 / 3                                                                              4213 / Aircraft Support Equipment                                                   1 / LAMPS MK III - SRQ(KU)-4 (S1010)
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready) :                                                                                  MDAP/MAIS Code:
                             Resource Summary                                         Prior Years                 FY 2024                   FY 2025                  FY 2026 Base       FY 2026 OOC       FY 2026 Total
Procurement Quantity (Units in Each)                                                                    -                         -                         -                     -                 -                           -
Gross/Weapon System Cost ($ in Millions)                                                           185.624                    14.233                   14.479                   0.000             0.000                   0.000
Less PY Advance Procurement ($ in Millions)                                                             -                         -                         -                     -                 -                           -
Net Procurement (P-1) ($ in Millions)                                                              185.624                    14.233                   14.479                   0.000             0.000                   0.000
Plus CY Advance Procurement ($ in Millions)                                                             -                         -                         -                     -                 -                           -

                            (The following Resource Summary rows are for informational purposes only. The corresponding budget requests are documented elsewhere.)
Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                     -                 -                           -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                     -                 -                           -



 Description:
 LAMPS MK III, AN/SRQ-4 (Ku), is an over the horizon information dominance system with a high speed, air-to-ground, digital data link that transmits data from MH-60 helicopters to surface ships (cruisers and
 destroyers) to enable data, imagery, acoustic, electronic support measures, communications and radar information via the Ku-band link.

 Program provides for non-recurring engineering and associated installation and support of AN/SRQ-4(Ku) field install kits. This system encompasses hardware and software to transmit sensor data from the Light
 Airborne Multi-Purpose System (LAMPS) MK III MH-60R aircraft to the host ship classes. Kit procurements were complete in FY 2021.

 Objective Inventory is 100 kits. (68 Destroyers [DDGs], 22 Cruisers [CGs], & 10 Shore sites). 22 kits were previously installed in line item 4255 and 78 kits are planned for installation in line item 4213. Seven of
 the kits in "To Complete" represent pending decommissioning of DDGs and CGs.

 Notes for installation schedule:
 1. Installations are subject to changes with ship availability schedules.

 2. The year-to-year unit installation cost varies significantly and can exceed the projected yearly inflation rate. The varying unit cost is due to the hardware installation cost being dependent on quantity, location
 (i.e., Japan, Norfolk, San Diego, etc.), Shipyard (i.e. Bath or Ingalls), ship baseline configuration (i.e., Navigation System installed, A-Kit pre-install), ship availability (AVAIL) period overlaps, complexity of AVAIL
 (e.g., SHIPALTs can last up to 3 yrs and require intermittent visits by the install team) as well as cost sharing from other simultaneous installed SHIPALTS (e.g. mast rigging).

 Footnotes:
 (40)
        Installs: Reconciliation of actual ship installations has resulted in ten installs in "to complete". Seven ships pending Decommissioning and part of the "To Complete" installs.
 (41)
     Cost Element 2.1, Data funding procures ship installation drawings and provides for ship checks on multiple Cruiser (CG) and Destroyers (DDG) class ships scheduled for SHIPALT 79719. This cost element
 provides the funds to procure the data packages eighteen months prior to ship installation start. Data costs vary year to year due to CG packages being significantly more expensive as compared to DDGs due to
 the age and individuality of the CGs.
 (42)
      Cost Element 2.2, Support Equipment is for the procurement of KU-Band equipment for the fleet to replace the Test Set-4120s that allows trouble shooting of the LAMPS MK III system without an embarked
 helicopter.
 (43)
     Cost Element 2.4, Government Engineering, is required for engineering support for kitting, staging, configuration/documentation management, procurement of GFE & support equipment, scheduling of ship
 installation activities, and test support required to produce, deliver and install the SRQ(KU)-4 kits onboard CG and DDG class ships. FY25 increase is required to support the installation efforts which continue into
 FY26 as installs are phased based on other concurrent ship activity/installs and ship checks. Close out activity required.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                      -                 -                  -



 Description:
 CMV-22 SCD is a Turn-Key Mod which will modify CVN ships to accommodate CMV-22 aircraft as the Carrier On-board Delivery (COD) aircraft replacing the C-2. Ship modifications have been approved via the
 following Ship Change Documents (SCD):

 1) SCD 7233 CMV-22 Fire Wand Clips: Mounting brackets required to secure fire wand extensions with attached 15lb CO2 bottle on CVNs in the vicinity of Spot 6, Spot 9, and the fire hose station at the base of
 the tower aft of the island.

 2) SCD 25268 CMV-22 Blade Storage: Angle arms to provide storage of V-22 spare blades. Two sets of angle arms will be installed to support bulkhead storage of 6 spare blades. CMV-22 blade storage will be
 in vicinity of frame 190 (Hangar Bay 3, CVN 68 Class - Hangar Bay 2, CVN 78 Class).

 3) SCD 25369 CMV-22 CVN Spaces Modifications: Current VRC C-2A (R) Detachments deployed to a CVN do not have spaces assigned, but share with the embarked E2C squadron. Due to the nature of the
 CONOPS, future VRM (CMV-22) Detachments deployed to CVN's will require spaces assigned to them for both the transit and operational periods of the deployment. This SCD realigns/installs infrastructure
 and configures spaces required to support the embarked footprint of a CMV-22 detachment as the Carrier On-board Delivery (COD) platform. These spaces include a Ready Room, Maintenance Control, and
 Maintenance Work Center.

 Funding beginning in FY 2023 will complete ship modifications for CVN 68, CVN 76, and CVN 69.

 Installations are subject to change with ship availability schedules.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -         -               -


 Description:
 This budget line item funds the procurement for Advanced Arresting Gear (AAG) life cycle support modifications and upgrades required aboard Ford Class Carriers, Jet Car Track Site (JCTS) and Runway
 Arrested Landing Site (RALS) systems. Modifications included in the budget support fatigue life, obsolescence and performance enhancements to ensure the system remains operational throughout the life
 of the program. AAG replaces Mark 7 arresting gear aboard Ford Class aircraft carriers and provides the U.S. Navy with the ability to recover existing and projected aircraft carrier based tail-hook equipped air
 vehicles well into the 21st century. AAG enhances operational capability, provides increased operational availability, reduces manning, maintenance and support costs. AAG is currently installed aboard CVN-78
 as well as two land based test sites JCTS and RALS.

 Prior Years ($76.9M) funded under Aircraft Support Equipment (BLI 4213 and BLI 4216).
