Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 FY 2026 Baseline funding procures and installs modification kits of the Cable Shock Absorber (CSA) Vent Valve Engineering Change Proposals (ECPs) and associated support. This ECP specifically addresses
 key operational degraders that are currently negatively impacting CVN-78 Operational Availability (Ao) and Reliability.

 FY 2026 Baseline funding also procures Support Equipment required for, and specific to, initial stand-up of AAG depot level repair capabilities for Ford Class ships. This includes AAG test fixtures, test stands
 and tool sets for component repair, with focus on capability for high failure components. In addition, FY 2026 Baseline funding procures support equipment required for, and specific to, AAG Software Support
 Activity (SSA) to make minor improvements and expand capabilities of the existing AAG lab. Stand-up of AAG Depot and SSA are required to establish capability and capacity to support multiple Ford class ships
 by 2028.

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $.038 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Notes for Installation Schedule:
 1. Installations are subject to change with ship availability schedule updates.
 2. The year-to-year unit installations costs will vary and can exceed the projected yearly inflation rate due to the hardware installation cost being dependent on quantity and location of the ship at the time of
 install or which site is being update, which CVN, and the ships baseline configuration.
 3. Production lead time includes pre-installation checkout, kitting with other installation hardware and drawings, and time to deliver the hardware to the installation site.

 Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) funding levels will fluctuate due to mix and complexity of system ECPs being written and verified in each year. All ECPs do not require the same level of NRE. Publications and
 Integrated Logistics Support also vary from year to year based on complexity of logistics elements and number of publications required to be updated for each individual ECP.

 Integrated Logistics Support and Production Engineering Support also varies from year to year based on complexity of logistics and engineering elements required for each individual ECP.

 Description:
 This budget line item funds the procurement for Advanced Arresting Gear (AAG) life cycle support modifications and upgrades required aboard Ford Class Carriers, Jet Car Track Site (JCTS) and Runway
 Arrested Landing Site (RALS) systems. Modifications included in the budget support fatigue life, obsolescence and performance enhancements to ensure the system remains operational throughout the life of
 the program. AAG will replace Mark 7 arresting gear aboard Ford Class aircraft carriers and will provide the U.S. Navy with the ability to recover existing and projected aircraft carrier based tail-hook equipped air
 vehicles well into the 21st century. AAG will enhance operational capability, provide increased operational availability, while reducing manning, maintenance and support costs. AAG is currently installed aboard
 CVN-78 and in two land based test sites at JCTS and RALS.

 Outyear TBD Reliability Improvement ECPs will be determined closer to year of execution to provide the program more flexibility to address competing priorities of what become the most critical obsolescence,
 reliability, operational, or safety upgrades.

 FY 2017 and prior funded under Aircraft Support Equipment (BLI 4213 and BLI 4216).

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    1.1.2) Ten Water Twister Mod II kits were procured with prior year funds (2 units FY20 RDTE, 2 units FY21 OPN-3, 4 units FY22 OPN-3, 2 units FY23 OPN-3), and two kits were procured with FY24 OPN-3
 funds. At the time of contract award, installation in CVN-79 was planned for FY30, after ship delivery in FY25. However, demonstrated failures of the Pie Plate bolts associated with Mod I Water Twisters in
 CVN-78 required the program to accelerate procurement and install the Mod II Water Twisters prior to CVN-79 ship delivery to reduce the risk of degraded flight ops. Due to this acceleration, installation aboard
 CVN-79 is funded with SCN.
 (2)
    1.1.3) Repeated compression of the Cable Shock Absorber (CSA) pistons causes trapped air to build up within the CSA hydraulic fluid, causing degraded performance. The CSA Vent Valve Incorporation
 reduces maintenance and pre-operation set-up time by enabling AAG maintainers to quickly and safely release trapped air from within the CSA hydraulic cylinder during Pre-Operations before every flight
 operation period.
 (3)
        2.4) Support Equipment increases from FY 2025 to FY 2026 for procurement of equipment required for AAG Depot Stand-up and Software Support Activity (SSA) activities.
 (4)
        A quantity of (6) WT Mod II installations must be contracted for in FY2025 due to the NAVSEA Ship Change process, which requires installation work to be on contract six months prior to ship availability.

 Description:
 The Electromagnetic Aircraft Launch System (EMALS) is replacing the C13 Steam Catapult System aboard Ford Class carriers and is an advanced technology aircraft launch system which provides improved
 reliability and maintainability, reduced manning workload and increased operational availability.

 This budget line-item funds the procurement for Electromagnetic Aircraft Launch System (EMALS) life cycle support modifications and upgrades required aboard Ford Class carriers and System Functional
 Demonstration (SFD) land-based test site. Modifications included in the budget support fatigue life, obsolescence and performance enhancements to ensure the system remains operational throughout the life of
 the program. EMALS replaces the C13 Steam Catapult System aboard Ford Class carriers and is an advanced technology aircraft launch system which provides improved reliability and maintainability, reduced
 manning workload and increased operational availability. EMALS is installed aboard CVN-78 and in one land-based test site at SFD.

 EMALS prior years ($39.910M) was previously funded under Aircraft Support Equipment (BLI 4213 and 4216).

Note: Totals in this Exhibit P-40 set may not be exact or sum exactly due to rounding.

 Justification:
 FY 2026 Baseline funding is required for Depot Standup, installation of modifications for Transformer Rectifier (T/R) Fuse Detection, and modifications based on obsolescence. Work categories include
 Production Engineering (PE), Integrated Logistics Support (ILS), and Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE).

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $.180 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Depot Standup Support Equipment is to procure equipment required for initial standup of EMALS depot level repair capabilities for Ford Class ships. This includes final procurement of logistics products,
 procurement of on-equipment tooling, test fixtures, test stands, and OEM manufactured tooling to support upcoming planned overhaul periodicities. In addition, procuring Detailed Functional Description Data
 Packages (DFDDPs) to support design and procurement of future test benches focusing on high power and low power testing, troubleshooting, and fault diagnostic capability for component repair. Increase from
 FY 2025 to FY 2026 due to the phased approach in each year. Software Support Activity (SSA) Support Equipment is required to make minor improvements and expand capabilities of the existing EMALS lab.
 Standup of EMALS Depot and SSA are required to establish capability and capacity to support four Ford Class ships by 2028.

 FY 2026 Installations for T/R modifications will be accomplished during Pier Side Ship availabilities.

 Notes for Installation Schedule:
 1. Installations are subject to change with ship availability schedule updates.
 2. The year-to-year unit installations costs will vary and can exceed the projected yearly inflation rate due to the hardware installation cost being dependent on quantity and location of the ship at the time of install
 or which site is
 being update, which CVN, and the ships baseline configuration.
 3. Production lead time includes pre-installation checkout, kitting with other installation hardware and drawings, and time to deliver the hardware to the installation site.

 Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) funding levels fluctuate due to a mix and complexity of system Engineering Change Proposals (ECP) written and verified in each year. All ECPs do not require the same level of
 NRE.

 Integrated Logistics Support also varies from year to year based on complexity of logistics elements required for each individual ECP.

 Description:
 This budget line-item funds the procurement for Electromagnetic Aircraft Launch System (EMALS) life cycle support modifications and upgrades required aboard Ford Class carriers and System Functional
 Demonstration (SFD) land-based test site. Modifications included in the budget support fatigue life, obsolescence and performance enhancements to ensure the system remains operational throughout the life of
 the program. EMALS replaces the C13 Steam Catapult System aboard Ford Class carriers and is an advanced technology aircraft launch system which provides improved reliability and maintainability, reduced
 manning workload and increased operational availability. EMALS is installed aboard CVN-78 and in one land-based test site at SFD.

 EMALS ECP upgrades include four (4) major categories of upgrades:
 (1) Obsolescence: provides funding for ECPs used to address critical or obsolete parts in any of the major or subcomponent systems and emergent obsolescence/tech refresh within EMALS.
 (2) Reliability Improvements/Upgrades: provides funding for ECPs to address any issues that support reliability of EMALS system at SFD and onboard Ford Class ships.
 (3) Operational Capability Improvements: provides funding for ECPs that specifically address operational capability improvements, which are mostly driven by changes as a result of findings during Correction of
 Deficiency testing.
 (4) Safety of Operations/Flight: provides funding for ECPs that directly affect safety of flight operations that might limit flight operations during normal optempo.
 Additionally, this budget includes ECPs for items such as software upgrades to components or subsystem components that cause issues under normal operating conditions of EMALS will be included in each of
 the four categories when software updates are required. Funding is also used to support directed cyber mandates to ensure compliance of cyber issues in EMALS operating system.

 Planned EMALS ECPs are subject to change in the year of execution due to urgent and compelling fleet requirements in support of Ford Class carrier operational commitments.

 Outyear ECPs will be determined closer to year of execution to provide the program more flexibility to address competing priorities of what becomes the most critical obsolescence, reliability, operational, or
 safety upgrades.

 E22 Build currently addresses: 1) hardware obsolescence for Half-Bridge IGBT (retrofit by attrition); 2) Block Switch Sensor (BSS) safety software; 3) Transformer Rectifier (T/R) Fuse Detection; 4) Network
 Switch; 5) Prime Power Interface Subsystem (PPIS) Inverter Software; 6) Motor Controller; and 7) MC PSB Stuck On Safety Software.

 Description:
 The Meteorological Equipment budget line item provides new and replacement meteorological equipment for Navy and Marine Corps Air Stations, Navy ships, Marine Corps Operational Force units and other
 activities required to provide weather observations and provide safety of flight capabilities. It also provides new Intelligence Preparation of the Operational Environment (IPOE) equipment for submarines. The
 Meteorological Equipment procurement has been coordinated with other Department of Defense (DoD) and civilian agencies.

 [P40A / SP051 Satellite Receiver Upgrades (Space)]: Environmental Satellite Receiver Processor Systems (ESRP Ashore and ESRP Afloat) receive and process remotely sensed data in L and S frequency
 bands from several National and International Meteorology and Oceanography (METOC) Satellite families such as the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program (DMSP) Polar Satellites, the National Oceanic
 and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Satellites, and the Geostationary Operational Environmental Satellite (GOES-R) Series. Integration of next generation polar and geostationary orbiting satellite families
 and new sensors of opportunity shall be incorporated in the hardware design and software upgrades to existing Environmental Satellite Receiver Processor Systems both afloat and ashore. The modernization of
 systems includes GOES-R, Electro-Optical/Infrared Weather Systems - Geostationary (EWS-G), and Weather System Follow-On Microwave (WSF-M) ingest of METOC data, as well as the addition of X and C
 frequency bands for current and future capabilities.

 [P40A / SP550 METOC SASC Upgrades]: Procures Government Off-The-Shelf/Commercial Off-The-Shelf hardware and associated software upgrades for the 69 fielded Automated Surface Observing Systems
 (ASOS) and the 9 fielded Supplemental Weather Radars (SWR). Both atmospheric sensing systems are essential for aviation safety, Naval Aviation operations, and resource protection. ASOS procurements
 under this project will provide system upgrades as required by the 2020 National Weather Service Interagency Agreement. SWR upgrades of End of Life components, software, and subsystems under this project
 provide System Life Extension by mitigating CYBER risk and Diminishing Manufacturing Sources and Material Shortages (DMSMS).

 [P40A / SP600 Littoral Battlespace Sensing - Unmanned Undersea Vehicles (LBS-UUV)]: Procures Unmanned Undersea Vehicle (UUV) ocean sensor systems. These include long duration (3 months) buoyancy
 driven Ocean Gliders that sense ocean volume parameters (conductivity, temperature, depth, optical clarity, currents, etc.). The Ocean Gliders are controlled remotely from the Naval Oceanographic Office
 (NAVOCEANO), Stennis Space Center, Mississippi from the Glider Operations Center (GOC). Quantities represent Glider replacements to reach inventory objective due to lost, stolen or damaged units and the
 Fleet's inability to retrieve all deployed units.

 [P40A / SP660 Tactical METOC Applications]: The Naval Integrated METOC Systems (NIMS), formerly Meteorological Mobile Facility Next Generation (MetMF - NextGen), provides meteorological and
 oceanographic (METOC) support to the Marine Corps and Joint forces. The primary functions of the systems are to collect, process and exploit, interpret, produce, and disseminate METOC data. Those functions
 are carried out through 6 integrated subsystems; Doppler Radar, Local & Remote Sensors, Meteorological Satellite, Upper Air Sensor, Communications, and Processing Computer Subsystem (PCS), which


LI 4226 - Meteorological Equipment                                                                       UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                     Volume 3 - 97
Navy                                                                                                             Page 1 of 7                                  P-1 Line #118
                                                                                               UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                        Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                      P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 3:                                 4226 / Meteorological Equipment
Aircraft Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                  Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 includes a Forecaster Tool Kit (FTK) that collects, processes, and transmits METOC data. In 2023, the Naval Integrated Tactical Environmental System - Next Generation (NITES - Next) hardware component,
 NITES IV, moved to the METMF Program. This move places Navy and Marine Corps hardware under one Program and expands the Warfare mission areas to include all aspects of the Marine Air Ground
 Task Force (MAGTF), Undersea Warfare (USW), Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW), Mine Warfare (MIW), Amphibious Warfare (AMW), Anti-Surface Warfare (ASUW), Anti-Air Warfare (AAW), Strike Warfare
 (STW), Expeditionary Warfare (EXW), Electronic Warfare (EW), Information Operations (IO), Intelligence Operations (INT), Non-Combat Operations (NCO), Command, Control, Communication (CCC), and
 Naval Special Warfare (NSW). The Program supports those missions by utilizing real time battlespace observed meteorological parameters; automatically displays, generates, and disseminates customized
 products; provides in-situ data through an onboard Mesoscale "NOWCAST" Modeling capability; provides Tactical Decision Aids (TDA's) for targeting, Electronic Counter Measures (ECM), and Electronic
 Counter-Counter Measures (ECCM) performance; provides decision makers with information that impacts mission effectiveness and weapon system performance; and operates on DoD NIPR, SIPRNET, ship
 and tactical networks. TDA's consist of a series of analysis tools which characterize the physical environmental conditions of the battlespace based on the best set of physical environmental data available at the
 time (i.e., some combination of historical and/or real-time (or near real-time) in-situ, and numerically modeled forecast data). This program addresses multi-warfare areas across Navy and Marine Corps mission
 requirements, particularly shallow water ASW, NSW, and USMC MAGTF.

 Beginning in FY 2023, the NITES-Next Program was absorbed into the Naval Integrated METOC Systems (NIMS) Program (formerly Meteorological Mobile Facility Next Generation (MetMF - NextGen). NIMS
 will be a more scalable configuration and absorbs the NITES program hardware requirement - laptops, servers and atmospheric surface sensors. The NIMS program satisfies Fleet Marine Force and Joint
 Force Meteorology and Oceanography (METOC) employment requirements via a system of organic atmospheric sensing sub-systems through the delivery of timely, accurate and relevant METOC products and
 mission impact assessments.

 [P40A / SP220 Hazardous Weather Detection and Display Capability (HWDDC)]: The Hazardous Weather Detection and Display Capability (HWDDC) provides near real-time severe weather information
 (thunderstorms, high winds, turbulence, etc.) to ship, Strike Group, and Expeditionary Group personnel. HWDDC is a key safety of aviation and navigation enabler. HWDDC also supports efficient planning and
 execution of aircraft and small boat launch/recovery operations, as well as severe weather dependent evolutions such as underway replenishments. The HWDDC technology is integrated with the AN/SPS-48E
 and AN/SPS-48G radars. HWDDC is currently installed on all Force Level ships (except CVN-78) with Fleet requirements for AN/SPY 6 Enterprise Air Surveillance Radar (EASR) hardware End of Life component
 upgrades, and associated software upgrades to enhance capabilities and prevent cyber vulnerabilities in the Fleet.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.828 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.282 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    ESRP Afloat Modernized System - Primary focus for the FY 2026 request is for procurement and installations of the modernized Satellite Receiver as Production Representative Assets (PRA) at an ECH III CB-ISEA location and on afloat
 platforms in accordance with the programs budgeted quantities in support of the Meteorological and Oceanographic Afloat and Surface-based Atmospheric Sensing Capability (METOC SASC) family of systems. FY 2026 decrease is due to
 reduction of ESRP Afloat system procurements and installations from FY 2025 to FY 2026.
 (2)
    Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) Upgrades - FY 2026 decrease is due to the procurement of (2) fewer systems. FY 2026 unit cost increase is due to the procurement of fewer systems resulting in a decreased quantity buy
 discount.
 (3)
       LBS-Gliders (LBS-G) - FY 2026 decrease is due to the procurement of (3) fewer systems.
 (4)
       LBS-UUV Upgrades/Backfits - FY 2026 decrease is due to the procurement of (1) fewer system.
 (5)
   Naval Integrated METOC Systems (NIMS) Sensors (formerly Meteorological Mobile Facility Next Generation (MetMF - NextGen) - FY 2026 decrease due to change in requirements to reduce Naval Expeditionary environmental support to
 modernize Marine Corps systems. FY 2026 unit cost increase is due to the procurement of (26) fewer systems resulting in a decreased quantity buy discount.
 (6)
       FY 2026 ESRP Afloat system installation decrease is due to the ESRP Afloat system reduction in installation quantities from FY 2025 to FY 2026. This decrease also reflects the deferral of one (1) installation in FY26.
 (7)
       HWDDC (DSA) - FY 2026 increase due to installation of (1) additional systems in alignment with the fielding plan.
 (8)
    Consolidated Prior Year Requirements: Programs included in this Cost Element include: TESS/NITES (Mobile Tactical System and Hardware Components, METMF(R) NEXGEN/Subsystems, METOC Satellite Data Exploitation Readiness

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -         -            -


 Description:
 Airborne Mine Countermeasures (AMCM) Equipment is currently deployed on MH-60S and MH-53E helicopters to counter the threat of sea mines. The Navy's MH-53E helicopter and its associated
 AMCM Systems are intended to be divested through FY 2028. This divestment is associated with fulfillment of the National Defense Authorization Act for FY 2018 by the Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) Mine
 Countermeasures (MCM) Mission Package (MP).

 Fielding and first deployment of the MCM MP onboard LCS occurred in FY 2025. Fleet usage has increased resulting in ramp-up of sustainment and depot activities starting in FY 2024 for MH-60S MCM
 systems. In-Service teams are addressing obsolescence and AMCM system updates. The equipment is divided into two categories -- minehunting and mine neutralization.

 In minehunting, mine-like objects are located and classified (usually by means of high-resolution sonar or optical systems). The fleeted system for this task is AN/AES-1 Airborne Laser Mine Detection System
 (ALMDS) on the MH-60S helicopter. Finally, mines are neutralized using explosive devices. This includes the AN/ASQ-235 Archerfish neutralizer on the MH-60S.

 Current world mining threats have resulted in increased operational demand of systems to perform AMCM missions. Fleets require increased AMCM capability to address warfighting gaps and the reliability,
 maintainability, and availability of critical systems to perform world-wide operations. The AMCM program provides systems to address a global threat and serves to deter placement of sea mines. Lack of AMCM
 capability will result in loss of the Fleet's ability to conduct freedom of maneuver and poses increased risk to ships operating in mine threat areas.

 In addition, the MEDAL MINEnet Tactical (MNT), Minefield Planning (MFP), and NSAM are software systems that have associated hardware components. MINEnet Tactical (MNT) is the Navy's single mine
 warfare (MIW) tactical decision aid (TDA) that provides tactical planning, post-mission evaluation, and situational awareness. Minefield Planning (MFP) is software that provides users the ability to plan and
 assess minefields based on user specified objective, targets, and environmental data. Net-Centric Sensor Analysis for MIW (NSAM) replaced the Organic Post Mission Analysis (OPMA) system and provides
 post mission analysis (PMA) capabilities for the Airborne Laser Mine Detection System (ALMDS) and the Airborne Mine Neutralization System (AMNS) and integrated contact management capabilities for
 ALMDS, AMNS, AN/AQS-20C.

 [P5 / MODIFICATIONS]: Funds support the modification and product improvement of AMCM systems to accommodate replacement of subsystems/components because of safety, maintainability, reliability,
 and obsolescence issues. Engineering Change Proposals (ECPs) generated from obsolescence issues and parts failures are necessary to sustain the operational availability and reliability of the Fleet's AMCM
 program through end of service life. Additionally, as the systems have aged, operational and intermediate level maintenance actions on the systems have failed to return assets to Ready for Issue (RFI) condition.
 Depot level overhauls of these systems and components are required to ensure the systems are sustainable through the end of their service life. ECPs are analyzed, prioritized, and screened to accommodate


LI 4248 - Airborne MCM                                                                                  UNCLASSIFIED

Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                    P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 3:                               4248 / Airborne MCM
Aircraft Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                      Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                 Other Related Program Elements: 0604373N
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 replacement of subsystems/components. Funds will also support upgrades of hardware to maintain their system's operational availability as more demanding operational support and software requirements are
 levied on aging hardware. Cybersecurity upgrades are required to ensure compliance with Department of Navy's Risk Management Framework process.

 - AN/ASQ-235 Airborne Mine Neutralization System (AMNS): Funds support a mine neutralization system that provides a remotely controlled expendable mine neutralization device deployed from the MH-60S
 helicopter platform to reacquire, identify, and neutralize moored and proud sea mines. Updates are planned for the Launch and Handling System (LHS).

 - AN/AES-1 Airborne Laser Mine Detection System (ALMDS): Funds support a mine hunting system that provides organic rapid wide-area reconnaissance and assessment of mine threats in littoral zones,
 confined straits, choke points, and amphibious objective areas for Carrier Strike Groups and Expeditionary Strike Groups. ALMDS is a light detection and ranging (LIDAR) system for high area coverage, rapid
 detection, classification, and localization of near surface and moored sea mines operating from the MH-60S helicopter platform. This funding line supports modification and product improvements of ALMDS
 to address obsolescence issues of a 20-year-old design. These include, but are not limited to those identified in the receiver, laser, and the Central Electronics Chassis (CEC) to ensure the systems are
 sustainable through the end of their service life which is projected through 2045. Modernization efforts will contribute to False Call Reduction using Machine Learning and continue software modifications to
 improve simulation software and cybersecurity.

 [P5 - 2 / NSAM]: Net-Centric Sensor Analysis for MIW (NSAM) replaced the Organic Post Mission Analysis (OPMA) system and NSAM provides post mission analysis (PMA) capabilities for the Airborne Laser
 Mine Detection System (ALMDS) and the Airborne Mine Neutralization System (AMNS) and integrated contact management. Additionally, NSAM will replace the AN/AQS-20C interim Post Mission Analysis
 (iPMA) system. NSAM will be the single tactical and environmental PMA system for all future Mine Warfare (MIW) sensor data and will provide integrated contact management capabilities.

 [P5 - 2 / Hardware]: FY 2024-FY 2026 NSAM funding supports hardware procurement to address Diminishing Manufacturing Sources and Material Shortages (DMSMS), obsolescence issues, and processing
 power requirements. Funding supports partial replacement of existing hardware.

 [P5 - 3 / MINEnet Tactical (MNT)]: FY2026-FY2027 funding procures MNT hardware and spares to replace obsolete computers and remain cybersecurity compliant. The funding also includes associated labor
 costs.

 [P5 - 4 / Minefield Planning (MFP)]: FY2026-FY2027 funds procure hardware for deployment and spares.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.573 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."

 1 / MODIFICATIONS:
 FY 2026 funding supports modifications, product improvements, and engineering change proposals (ECPs) of the AN/AES-1 Airborne Laser Mine Detection System (ALMDS) and AN/ASQ-235 Airborne Mine
 Neutralization System (AMNS) Launch and Handling System (LHS). Flight hours for ALMDS and AMNS are expected to increase due to these systems replacing the legacy systems. Both of these AMCM
 systems entered service in 2017 and a number of components have been identified for replacement due to obsolescence. Replacement will improve reliability, availability, and performance. In addition to the
 ongoing ECPs and modernization of the ALMDS Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) algorithm, ALMDS is refurbishing its Laser Transmitter Unit (LTU) coolant o-rings. This repair requires all 29 LTUs to
 undergo refurbishments.

 2/ NSAM:
 FY 2024-2026 NSAM funding supports procurement of NSAM computers.

 3/ MNT:
 FY2026-FY2027 funding procures MNT hardware and spares to replace obsolete computers and remain cybersecurity compliant, in addition to associated labor costs.

 4/ MFP:
 FY2026-FY2027 funds procure hardware for deployment and spares.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                               -           1.202           1.279          2.025             -            2.025             -               -         -            -     -             -
Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -             -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -             -


 Description:
 [P40A / S7039 - Aviation Data Warehouse Environment]: Decision Knowledge Programming for Logistics Analysis and Technical Evaluation (DECKPLATE) (S7039): DECKPLATE is the next generation of Naval
 Aviation Logistics Data Analysis (NALDA) and will interface with Navy Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) data and Logistics IT source systems as the Naval Aviation Business Warehouse. It provides the
 technological improvements and process streamlining required to enable a cost wise transition from the NALDA program to the capabilities required in the Navy Digital Transformation Road Map. DECKPLATE is
 a commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) intensive system under which numerous stovepipe legacy systems will migrate to create an integrated data environment through the use of Data Warehouse tools and concepts
 in support of Naval aviation logistics needs. This is being accomplished by upgrading current Naval Aviation logistics reporting mechanisms through the procurement and installation of a fully-licensed, warranted,
 secure, standardized, COTS, user-friendly, web-based relational database environment. Funding is required to procure the necessary hardware, networking, systems, applications software, infrastructure, and
 associated engineering and installation support.

 [P40A / S7041 - NFSA HW/SW]: Naval Aviation Logistics Command Management Information System/NAVAIR Fleet System Array (NALCOMIS/NFSA): Funding supports procurement of hardware/software for
 technical refresh for both shipboard and afloat units. NALCOMIS (also identified as Naval Air Systems Command Fleet Systems Array (NFSA)) is responsible for implementation of Mid-Tier Servers at 55 sites
 both shipboard and shore based. These Mid-Tier Servers replicate data from the Organizational and Intermediate level maintenance activities to DECKPLATE to provide near-real time data to decision makers at
 all levels. The Mid-Tier also allows data to be pushed from Headquarters activities to the fleet to support maintenance activities.

 [P40A / S7042 - JTDI HW/SW]: Joint Technical Data Integration (JTDI): Funding supports procurement of JTDI for installation on all Carrier (CV) and Amphibious Assault (L) class ships and up to Navy/Marine
 Corps aviation activities. JTDI is a digital technical data access, delivery and local Organizational and Intermediate (O&I) level library management toolset and enabler of condition-based maintenance processes.
 It improves accuracy and timeliness of weapon system repair manuals and other technical data delivery and minimizes the Fleet's library management burden. JTDI reduces maintenance man hours with
 savings Return on Investment (ROI) of 2.5:1 and savings/avoidance ROI of 9.5:1. JTDI also automates the movement of Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) data generated by smart weapon systems
 deployed around the globe, consolidates and makes platform sensor data available for automated ingest into the Standard Data Repository which provides modern, highly integrated analytic capabilities to enable
 condition-based maintenance processes, improve aircraft readiness, and identify opportunities to reduce weapon system maintenance costs. JTDI data transport capabilities support the future state for Logistics
 IT and Readiness: Naval Product Lifecycle Management (N-PLM), Naval Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (N-MRO), Naval Supply Chain Management (N-SCM), and Integrated Data Environment (IDE).

 [P40A / S7048 - CBM+ HW/SW]: Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) (S7048): Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) The CBM+ solution is an initiative which provides Naval Aviation Enterprise
 with common enabling capabilities which deliver timely data-driven decisional information to optimize aircraft availability and materiel readiness by incorporating health and usage leading indicators into the
 failure mode mitigation process. These CBM capabilities enable the Warfighters to more efficiently meet mission requirements through automated analysis and decision making processes. The CBM+ Initiative


LI 4268 - Aviation Support Equipment                                                                    UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                    Volume 3 - 113
Navy                                                                                                           Page 1 of 27                                  P-1 Line #121
                                                                                                 UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                             Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                         P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 3:                                    4268 / Aviation Support Equipment
Aircraft Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                         Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                    Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 increases readiness through streamlined maintenance processes, actionable logistics/engineering data and integrated analytics not previously available, enabling engineers and acquisition professionals to
 support system improvements based on CBM+ acquired data and analytic results. CBM+ provides the required infrastructure and storage solutions within an Enterprise common environment needed to store
 and analyze weapon system sensor data to extend the life of current and new acquisition aircraft, while scaling CBM+ across NAVAIR programs and realizing savings from reductions in field (organizational and
 intermediate) maintenance actions, reduced functional check flight hours, mishap mitigation, and reduced parts usage.

 [P40A / S7049-Digital Production Floor]: Commander Fleet Readiness Center (COMFRC) Digital Production Floor (DPF): DPF initiative brings Navy Aviation Depots into the 21st Century by removing paper from
 the production floor and integrating key quality elements to support a true digital North Star to ensure viability and alignment with broader Naval Logistics IT (LOG-IT) enterprise initiatives to realize a fully unified
 digital sustainment capability. This capability aligns and leverages ongoing Digital Thread /Aviation Product Lifecycle Management (AvPLM) efforts to transform our existing way of doing business and align us
 with commercial best practices for digitization of business processes. Current paper based processes have demonstrated inefficiencies and administrative delays in performance and quality of product on Depot
 level repair turnaround times.

 [P40A / S7050-Digital Toolboxes]: Commander Fleet Readiness Center (COMFRC) Digital Toolboxes: In alignment with the COMFRC Digital Production Floor (DPF) and broader Naval Logistics IT (LOG-
 IT) enterprise initiatives, this initiative will deploy standardized digital tool boxes to Navy Aviation Depots. This capability aligns and leverages ongoing Digital Thread /Aviation Product Lifecycle Management
 (AvPLM) efforts to transform our existing way of doing business and align us with commercial best practices for digitization of business processes. In addition to fielding the tool boxes, the intent is to implement
 common business methodologies across Depot activities, subsequently capturing the data collected into the LOG-IT enterprise architecture for iterative planning, analysis, reporting and metrics supporting
 leadership objectives.

 [P40A / Inactive Projects]: Inactive projects prior year totals.

 [P5 / AHP - SY520]: NOISE REDUCTION/AIRCREW HEARING PROTECTION (AHP) - SY520 (Baseline)
 Hearing loss is a significant cost driver across DoD in veteran disability compensation. Noise levels in Naval Aviation environments present a risk to permanent hearing loss in aircrew and aviation maintainers,
 if not mitigated. Poor communications intelligibility in loud environments increases risk to safety of operations and mission effectiveness. The AHP program efficiently aligns with a Common Fixed Wing (FW)
 helmet program being pursued to reduce weight and improve center of gravity, reducing back and neck strain (and mount an improved mask/regulator for FW aircrew).

 [P5 / RLD - SY200]: AIRCREW RECOVERY LOCATOR DEVICE (RLD) - SY200 (Baseline)
 Supports the procurement and fielding of a small, auto-activated, precise signaling device that transmits a secure distress signal in a GPS-denied environment over existing U.S. government and other
 communications architectures. Provides a secure emergency beacon for aircrew to support active Search and Rescue forces.

 [P5 / SVSI - SY180]: ROTARY WING / TILT ROTOR SURVIVAL VEST SYSTEMS IMPROVEMENT (SVSI) - SY180 (Baseline)
 The improved SVSI Ensemble addresses safety and performance issues with the currently fielded vest system. Supports the procurements of new survival vest and reduction or miniaturization of body-mounted
 Aviation Life Support Systems.

 [P5 / IJHMCS - SY215]: IMPROVED JOINT HELMET MOUNTED CUEING SYSTEM (IJHMCS) - SY215 (Baseline)
 Supports the procurement of the upgraded helmet mounted solution for the F/A-18E/F and EA-18G aircraft. IJHMCS will mitigate aircrew back and neck strain/fatigue and replace the JHMCS Helmet Mounted
 Day Display and Night Vision Cueing Device (NVCD) with a single day/night, digital color-capable helmet-mounted display.

 [P5 / HPH - SY505]: HEARING PROTECTION HELMET (HPH) - SY505 (Baseline)
 Hearing loss is a significant cost driver across DoD in veteran disability compensation. Noise levels in Naval Aviation environments present a risk for permanent hearing loss in aircrew and aviation maintainers
 if not mitigated. Joint efforts are underway to incrementally field improved technology solutions as they become available to better mitigate operator impacts. Increment 1 Double Hearing Protection (DHP)
 solutions were developed for aviation maintainers and began fielding in 2011. Increment 2 Triple Hearing Protection (THP) solutions will further improve noise reduction for more challenging environments. THP
 variants also incorporate a flight deck communications capability. Increment 3 Hearing Protection Helmet (HPH) will address associated design trade improvements including weight reduction and center of


LI 4268 - Aviation Support Equipment                                                             UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                         Volume 3 - 114
Navy                                                                                                  Page 2 of 27                                  P-1 Line #121
                                                                                               UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                         Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                       P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 3:                                  4268 / Aviation Support Equipment
Aircraft Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                   Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 gravity adjustments to reduce neck stresses, improved impact liner, night vision/cueing mounts, and potential incorporation of maturing technologies. These technologies include active digital noise reduction/
 cancellation, Face-to-Face communication, F/A-18 and F-35 ICS Interface, and Flight Deck Radio/Sound Powered Phone (SPP) communication capability.

 [P5 / HMR - SY141]: HELMET/MASK REGULATOR (HMR) - SY141 (Baseline)
 HMR is a family of configuration items which, together, mitigate the risk of physiological episodes. Each configuration item; the helmet, the mask, and the regulator, will be procured separately as they are
 qualified. Quantity and unit cost are managed per configuration item.

 [P5 / HNIM - SY139]: MH-60R/S HEAD & NECK INJURY MITIGATION (HNIM) - SY139 (Baseline)
 HNIM incorporates vibration dampers into MH-60R/S cockpit seats and modifies rotary wing helmets and vests, reducing back bulk and weight and minimizing back and neck strain and fatigue. Quantity and unit
 cost will be finalized once a solution has been determined.

 [P5 / ISEA - SY143]: IMPROVED SURVIVAL EGRESS AIR BOTTLE (ISEA) - SY143 (Baseline)
 ISEA Bottle incorporates improved/lighter SEA bottles and regulators, reducing bulk and weight for required equipment for over-water flight operations. The goal is to mitigate the risk of acute injuries as well as
 chronic neck and back injuries. ISEA improves aircrew safety, readiness, and mission effectiveness.

 [P5 / PSE - SY144]: E-2D PARACHUTE SYSTEM ENSEMBLE (PSE) - SY144 (Baseline)
 PSE will update the currently fielded PSE to include modern technologies, reducing size, weight and accessibility for in-flight bladder relief. PSE upgrade will reduce fatigue during normal missions and increase
 ease of egress during bailout situations.

 [P5 / NGRWH - SY142]: NEXT GEN ROTARY WING HELMET (NGRWH) - SY142 (Baseline)
 Funds the development, test, qualification and fielding of the NGRWH with improved center of gravity and a decrease in overall weight and thermal burden. The improved helmet will also mitigate neck and back
 issues.

 [P5 / Phys Mon - SY127]: PHYSIOLOGICAL MONITORING (Phys Mon) - SY127 (Baseline)
 Directly addresses physiological episodes, by measuring human performance and alerting aircrew preventing injury, death, and/or damage to aircraft. The system will constantly monitor biometrics and pilot
 performance to identify state of health or performance degradation that may result in loss of consciousness or ability to safely conduct the flight. This system will provide the operator with enough advance
 warning to accurately assess the situation and safely recover the aircraft. Multiple potential solutions have been evaluated to select a best of breed or blended solution. Since the aircrew responses to
 physiological episodes are so diverse, the solutions' data computation and warning algorithms will need to be iteratively adjusted over time using extensive operator usage feedback data.

 [P5 / ASIP - SY126]: AIRCREW SYSTEMS IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM (ASIP) - SY126 (Baseline)
 ASIP is modeled after the platform Operational Safety Improvement Program (OSIP) concept, with the objective of supporting the human (aircrew and aviation maintainers) as the weapon system platform. The
 Aircrew Systems portfolio exceeds 1,000 products. The majority of these products are Aviation Life Support Systems/Equipment (ALSS/E) that directly affect aircrew and aviation maintainer personnel safety and
 survivability, both in combat and non-combat operations. Other systems enhance aircrew and maintainer performance as the human element of the total weapon platform by enabling effective warfighting at night
 and physical endurance over extended missions. As legacy products age and missions are modified to address evolving threats and tactics, Fleet operators identify several items each year that must be modified
 to ensure safety and survivability, and address obsolescence. ASIP funding enables the program office to proactively manage or rapidly react to discrepancies and deficiencies across a broad portfolio. ASIP
 provides the resources to correct such issues via lower-scope Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) efforts and Engineering Change Proposals (ECPs), and/or qualification and procurement of improved capability
 commercial off-the-shelf (COTS), Non-Developmental Items (NDI) from industry or other service counterparts. Correction and enhancement efforts range from minor adjustments of survival kit items (such as
 brighter, lighter, and longer-lasting illumination devices and signaling rescue devices) to expeditiously identify and field Aircrew Systems safety-related deficiencies. ASIP enables the program office to address
 smaller scope, but critical Fleet safety of flight issues. The ASIP model enables the program office to shift resources as necessary and be more agile in assessing and addressing issues. The assessment is
 based on Fleet prioritization of identified issues, the level of improvement that a potential solution can provide, and the degree of maturity of that solution (readiness to be fielded).

 [P5 - 2 / S6003 Aviation Maintenance Advancement Solution]: Aviation Maintenance Advancement Solutions (AMAS) program integrates the Portable Electronic Maintenance Aid (PEMA) with the Standard
 PEMA Cyber Solution (SPECS) programs. The AMAS program will continue to deliver PEMA capability by providing commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) hardware integrated with Standard PEMA Cyber Solution
 (SPECS) Government off-the-shelf (GOTS) software solution. AMAS creates an integrated, cost-efficient maintenance and logistics support system for the warfighter. PEMA hardware is an unclassified support
 equipment device used by maintenance personnel to perform maintenance, inspection, fault diagnosis, and repair tasks in direct support of a Weapon System. PEMA hardware serves as a host for Interactive
 Electronic Technical Manuals, Automated Logistics Environment and Diagnostic/Prognostic applications. SPECS software Information System takes advantage of advance information technologies available
 in areas of personal computer networks, data communications, automatic Technical Publication and Software Information Assurance Availability Alert (IAVA) distribution, Host Based Security System (HBSS)
 Common Access Card (CAC)/Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), Full Disk Encryption and Assured Compliance Assessment Solution (ACAS). The employment of these technologies and future Virtual Private
 Networking (VPN) enhancements for efficient data movement enables the warfighter to focus on the mission while realizing increased efficiencies, improved security compliance, and reduction in sustainment
 costs over time.

 [P3A / ALIS - Program Support]: Autonomic Logistics Information System (ALIS) Ship Integration - CVN, LHD, LHA: ALIS controls all aspects of F-35 mission planning, maintenance, logistics, and supply
 functions. Funding for ALIS Ship Integration efforts (Programmatic Support, Engineering Support Services, Material, and Installation efforts) will enable shipboard (CVN, LHD, LHA) modification, classified/
 unclassified network integration, the installation of ALIS-related shipboard equipment, ALIS security accreditation, and verification of ALIS operation and functionality to include the integration of ALIS with
 shipboard Command, Control, Communications and Computers & Intelligence (C4I) Networks and the Prognostic Health Management (PHM) downlink. At the completion of each installation, the respective
 ship's ALIS will enable the F-35 system to provide, at the appropriate security levels via Navy Local Area Networks (LANs)/Wide Area Networks (WANs), the ability to transfer time-sensitive data for logistics
 support, mission planning, mission execution, and mission debriefing.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $1.226 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.635 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."

 The FY 2026 Budget Request includes funding for the following ($111.334M):

 P-40A (S7042), $2.592M
 Other Aviation Support Equipment - the procurement of hardware and associated support costs for JTDI (S7042). Procurement of Defense Enterprise Computing Center data center and cloud services; COTS
 hardware and software in support of ship, shore, and expeditionary aviation activities; and cyber security and program support services. JTDI is the common, enterprise solution for transporting authoritative
 technical data and CBM+ data to/from globally deployed aviation fleet activities and provides a common repository for raw CBM+ data which eliminates the need for weapon system programs to develop and
 maintain duplicative, platform-unique IT solutions.

 P-40A (S7048), $0.261M
 Other Aviation Support Equipment - the procurement of hardware and associated support costs for CBM+ (S7048).
 Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) is a maintenance optimization program that can generate significant benefits through maintenance program resets, maintenance effectiveness reviews, and active
 reliability centered maintenance (RCM) strategies at the program level. Employed as an enduring cross-life-cycle maintenance strategy, the program offers a technical solution to reduce maintenance burdens,
 increase operational availability and mission reliability of monitored systems, assists with identifying enterprise-wide cost avoidance, and will reduce overall life cycle costs. As a function of the Rapid Sustainment
 Improvement Process (RSIP), the CBM+ system will be installed on three to five surface combatants for selected shipboard equipment. Sensors for these systems will be defined, procured and installed. Further,
 the CBM+ system will be integrated with the Naval Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (N-MRO) suite of programs onboard ship to facilitate on ship and off ship monitoring.

 P-40A (S7041), $2.651M
 Other Aviation Support Equipment - Naval Aviation Logistics Command Management Information System/Naval Air Systems Command Fleet System Array (NALCOMIS/NFSA) (S7041) consists of the purchase
 of multiple components, which includes servers, peripheral hardware and software rack mounted into one server suite. Funds are required to manage obsolescence, comply with Cyber Security mandates which
 would lead to IT system shutdown without adherence, and provide fleet data replication and analysis processes to monitor safety and readiness of the Fleet.

 P-40A (S7039), $1.868M
 Other Aviation Support Equipment - Naval Aviation Logistics Data Analysis Decision Knowledge Programming for Logistics Analysis and Technical Evaluation (DECKPLATE)(S7039) consists of the purchase
 of multiple components, which includes servers, peripheral hardware and software rack mounted into one server suite. Funds are required to manage obsolescence and comply with CYBER WARFARE/
 and fleet data replication and analysis processes used to monitor safety and readiness of the Fleet as well as the ability to comply with mandatory Information Security (IA) mandates, which would lead to IT
 system shutdown without adherence. Analysis of cloud solutions may be required as we modernize and align DECKPLATE to future warehousing requirements such as Vision 2020, the future Naval Aviation
 Maintenance System (NAMS), and the Automated Logistics Environment.

 P-40A (S7049), $0.951M
 Other Aviation Support Equipment - the procurement of hardware and associated support costs for Commander Fleet Readiness Center (COMFRC) Digital Production Floor (DPF).

 P-40A (S7833), $1.355M
 Other Aviation Support Equipment - Production Engineering Support provided in support of Naval Aviation Logistics Data Analysis Decision Knowledge Programming for Logistics Analysis and Technical
 Evaluation (DECKPLATE)(S7039), Joint Technical Data Integration (JTDI) (S7042), Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) (S7048), Naval Aviation Logistics Command Management Information System/
 NAVAIR Fleet System Array (NALCOMIS/NFSA) (S7041), and Commander Fleet Readiness Center (COMFRC) Digital Production Floor (DPF)(S7049).

 P-5/1, $79.957M (Baseline)
 Aviation Life Support - the procurement of hardware and associated support costs for:

 Hearing Protection Helmets (HPH): Protects maintainers from daily high-level noise exposure produced by F-35 and F/A-18 aircraft. Addresses temporary hearing damage and permanent hearing loss while
 increasing the resilience of flight line personnel and increasing their comprehension of radio or ICS transmissions for increased mission effectiveness.

 Improved Joint Helmet Mounted Cueing System (IJHMCS): Addresses legacy JHMCS obsolescence, reduces weight, improves center of gravity to mitigate chronic back and neck injury, and reduces injury risk
 during ejection. System technology refresh enables heads up display of aircraft tactical situational awareness enhancements programmed for the cockpit displays and replaces legacy JHMCS and Night Vision
 Cueing Device (NVCD) with a single day/night high-definition color capable display of critical flight parameters and tactical data.

 Head and Neck Injury Mitigation (HNIM): Incorporates vibration dampers into MH-60R/S cockpit seats and modifications of rotary wing helmets and vests, reducing back bulk and weight and minimizing back and
 neck strain and fatigue.

 RW/TR Survival Vest Systems Improvement (SVSI): Addresses safety and performance issues with the currently fielded vest system. Supports the procurements of new survival vest and reduction or
 miniaturization of body-mounted Aviation Life Support Systems. Back and neck injury mitigation has been the Aircrew Systems ENARG #1 priority for five years. Mitigates chronic back and neck injuries and
 provides a vest with an enhanced flotation system, reduced weight and bulk, enhanced armor protection effectiveness, mobility, thermal burden, and egress survivability.

 Aircrew Systems Improvement Program (ASIP): Supports the human (aircrew and aviation maintainers) as the weapon system platform that directly affects safety and survivability, both in combat and non-
 combat operation. Actual components to be determined during project selection in the year prior to execution.

 Physiological Monitoring (Phys Mon): Addresses Naval Aviation's #1 safety concern by measuring human performance and alerting aircrew, preventing injury, death, and/or damage to aircraft.

 Helmet Mask Regulator (HMR): Reduces the likelihood of the occurrence of physiological episodes with the fielding of an improved helmet, mask, and regulator. Each item alone reduces risk, but synergistic
 effects are expected when the family of configuration items are fielded.

 Aircrew Hearing Protection (AHP). Addresses DoD's largest disability, hearing loss, by providing next generation hearing protection that utilizes new material science and digital noise reduction technology.

                                                                                                UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-5, Cost Analysis: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                                            Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                            P-1 Line Item Number / Title:                                                     Item Number / Title [DODIC]:
1810N / 03 / 3                                                                    4268 / Aviation Support Equipment                                                 1 / Aviation Life Support
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready) :                                                                      MDAP/MAIS Code:
 Footnotes:
 (6)
    SY520 Noise Reduction/Aircrew Hearing Protection (AHP): AHP continues procurement of headset hearing protection in FY26 in accordance with the approved acquisition strategy and procurement
 schedule. Aircrew continue to suffer temporary and permanent hearing loss. Poor communications intelligibility in loud environments increases risk to safety of operations and mission effectiveness. Provides
 Noise Reduction technology and a digitally tuned ear-cup and an in-ear solution to address relevant frequencies/volumes for increased protection across various TMS.
 (7)
     SY180 Rotary Wing / Tilt Rotor Survival Vest Systems Improvement (SVSI): SVSI procurements in FY26 in accordance with the approved acquisition strategy and procurement schedule. Current design
 cited as a causal factor in loss of aircrew during egress (HSC-8 SIR, 2021). Back and neck injury mitigation has been the Aircrew Systems ENARG #1 priority for five years. Mitigates chronic back and neck
 injuries and provides a vest with an enhanced flotation system, reduced weight and bulk, enhanced armor protection effectiveness, mobility, thermal burden, and egress survivability. Funding required to meet
 IOC. Previous efforts to qualify COTS/GOTS solutions were not successful. Realignment of resources to Aircrew Systems Development RDT&E, BA 05 PE 0604264N to continue to support the design, test and
 qualify a new survival vest system.
 (8)
     SY215 Improved Joint Helmet Monted Cueing System (IJHMCS): IJHMCS continues procurement in FY26 in accordance with the approved acquisition strategy and procurement schedule. Mitigates aircrew
 back and neck strain/fatigue and replaces the JHMCS Helmet Mounted Day Display and Night Vision Cueing Device (NVCD) with a single day/night, digital color-capable helmet-mounted display. Provides critical
 flight parameters and expanded tactical data to improve aircrew performance and enhance operational and warfighting capability. FY26 IJHMCS change in quantity due to revised cost estimate which updated
 the unit cost to a more accurate amount. Prior totals on the IJHMCS line are from Night Cueing Vision Display (NVCD), which ended in FY20. NVCD is the predecessor solution for this capability requirement. A
 revised cost estimate changed the unit cost for this product. The quantities and unit costs for FY26 have been revised based on this cost estimate.
 (9)
    SY505 Hearing Protection Helmet (HPH): HPH continues procurement in FY26 in accordance with the approved acquisition strategy and procurement schedule. Hearing loss is a significant cost driver
 across DoD in veteran disability compensation. Hearing Protection Helmet (HPH) will address associated design trade improvements, including weight reduction and center of gravity adjustments to reduce
 neck stresses, improved impact liner, night vision/cueing mounts, and potential incorporation of maturing technologies. These technologies include active digital noise reduction/cancellation, Face-to-Face
 communication, F/A-18 and F-35 ICS Interface, and Flight Deck Radio/Sound Powered Phone (SPP) communication capability.
 (10)
      SY141 Helmet/Mask Regulator (HMR): HMR starts procurement in FY26 in accordance with the approved acquisition strategy and procurement schedule. The quantity provided each year is an average of
 the total number of the family of configuration items required. Actual quantities will be managed per configuration item. FY26 realignment to RDT&E to support developmental efforts for the regulator.
 (11)
     SY139 MH-60R/A Head & Neck Injury Mitigation (HNIM): HNIM continues procurement in FY26 in accordance with the approved acquisition strategy and procurement schedule. Provides for MH-60R/S
 cockpit seats that reduce aircraft vibrations through incorporation of active and passive vibration dampening.
 (12)
     SY143 Improved Survival Egress Air Bottle (ISEA): ISEA starts procurement in FY26 in accordance with the acquisition strategy and procurement schedule. ISEA provides for the qualification, procurement,
 and fielding of reduced weight bottles and regulators. The outcome is to mitigate the risk of acute injuries as well as chronic neck and back injuries. Provides improved/lighter SEA bottles and regulators to
 reduce bulkiness and weight of required equipment for over-water flight operations.
 (13)
     SY127 Phys Mon: The physiological monitoring program is a Congressional interest item and an established Naval Aviation Life Support requirement as mandated by the Physiological Episode Action
 Team. The variation in quantities and average unit cost vary each year, reflect the iterative integration of additive multiple sensors and additional capabilities from the expected baseline physiological monitor, and
 represents the initial estimate prior to test completion, data analysis, and sensor selection(s) based on fleet needs or requirements.
 (14)
     SY126 Aircrew Systems Improvement Program (ASIP): FY26 quantities are established from the annual Aircrew Enabler Naval Aviation Requirements Group (ENARG), a Fleet demand signal event,
 and System Safety Working Group. The ASIP model enables the program office to shift resources as necessary and be more agile in assessing and addressing issues. The assessment is based on Fleet
 prioritization of identified issues, the level of improvement that a potential solution can provide, and the degree of maturity of that solution (readiness to be fielded). ASIP resources ensure deficiencies that
 threaten Aircrew safety, survivability, and performance, such as passive signaling for downed aviators, assured floatation, and chronic back and neck injury mitigation, are addressed in a timely manner. FY26
 increase represents adjustment to a more accurate representation of expected annual ASIP costs.
 (15)
    Increase in production support costs for FY26 is commensurate with the budget increase for approved Aviation Support Equipment requirements and is directly attributable to the transition of the HMR and
 ISEA programs from RDT&E development to the procurement support activities in FY26 as stated in footnotes 10 and 12.
 (16)
        SY830 Life Support Systems (LSS): Funds labor supporting ASIP products.

 are shown as each. If the maximum quantity is between 10,000 and 999,999 all quantities are shown in thousands. If the maximum quantity is between 1,000,000 and 999,999,999 all quantities are shown in millions (rounded to the nearest

 Description:
 [ALIS - Program Support] Autonomic Logistics Information System (ALIS) Ship Integration - CVN, LHD, LHA: ALIS controls all aspects of F-35 mission planning, maintenance, logistics, and supply functions.
 Funding for ALIS Ship Integration efforts (Programmatic Support, Engineering Support Services, Material, and Installation efforts) will enable shipboard (CVN, LHD, LHA) modification, classified/unclassified
 network integration, the installation of ALIS-related shipboard equipment, ALIS security accreditation, and verification of ALIS operation and functionality to include the integration of ALIS with shipboard
 Command, Control, Communications and Computers & Intelligence (C4I) Networks and the Prognostic Health Management (PHM) downlink. At the completion of each installation, the respective ship's ALIS will
 enable the F-35 system to provide, at the appropriate security levels via Navy Local Area Networks (LANs)/Wide Area Networks (WANs), the ability to transfer time-sensitive data for logistics support, mission
 planning, mission execution, and mission debriefing.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -            -


 Description:
 This Budget Line Item funds The Unmanned Carrier Aviation (UCA) Mission Control System (UMCS) program. This program builds, integrates, installs, and sustains the systems (control station, communications,
 and networks) required to operate the MQ-25, Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA) and future unmanned systems, which comprises the Air Wing of the Future (AWOTF), afloat and ashore.

 The UMCS program is the system-of-systems required for MQ-25 vehicle and payload control, both shipboard and shore based. The UMCS consists of all ground and ship-based hardware, software, and
 networks associated with the planning and execution of flight operations and tactical missions, including the control stations (displays, computers, and servers), line-of-sight communications, and beyond line-
 of-sight satellite communications. The ground control station (GCS), designated as the MD-5, allows pilots to command and control the MQ-25 Air Vehicle using external Command, Control, Communication,
 Computers, and Intelligence (C4I) systems. The main component of the GCS is the Multi Domain Combat System (MDCX), which will support multiple classification levels, control of multiple unmanned aircraft,
 and improve MQ-25 interoperability with other DoD systems. UMCS modifies approximately 12 -15 spaces on the aircraft carrier (CVN), using the Ship Change Document (SCD) process, to install the GCS and
 ancillary systems needed for communications, video management, and integration with existing CVN networks. The NAVWAR C4I systems provide the CVN network paths for air vehicle and mission payload
 data using a variety of wideband and narrowband communication paths. UMCS modifies and integrates with Launch and Recovery systems on both NIMITZ and FORD class CVNs: Aircraft Launch and Recovery
 Equipment (ALRE) and Joint Precision Approach Landing Systems (JPALS). Through the Software Support Activity (SSA), UMCS maintains software for the GCS and ancillary systems and verifies integration
 with MQ-25 Air Vehicle unique software.

 The GCS has three variants: fixed shore (MD-5D), CVN (MD-5C), and mobile (MD-5E, Embarkable - RDT&E funded). The fixed shore and CVN variants will be permanent installations to support MQ-25
 operations. The GCS consists of the following components: Air Vehicle Pilot (AVP) workstations, server racks, network interface racks, integrated communication system (ICS), and software. The CVN variant
 leverages shore system components but differs slightly to account for the rugged CVN environment. The UMCS ancillary systems provide the necessary communication, data, and network integration paths
 that allow MQ-25 pilots to control the MQ-25 and exchange data with CVN systems. MQ-25 pilots receive situational awareness data from two sources: the Video Management System (VidMS) and Air Traffic
 Control (ATC) picture. The Unmanned Carrier Aviation Transport System (UTS), used both afloat and ashore, integrates operational networks to provide narrowband and wideband communications and it
 contains the Data Transfer System (DTS) which allows the mission plan to be loaded on the MQ-25. Narrowband communications are provided by the ARC-210 Radio Communication System (RCS) and the
 Digital Modular Radio (DMR), which is used to access the Mobile User Objective System (MUOS). Each of these systems have afloat and ashore variants. The ARC-210 Radio Communication System ship
 system has an additional equipment rack to integrate with antennas located on the mast. The Integrated communication system half-rack, only installed on CVNs, is the interface between the GCS and Shipboard
 Air Traffic Control Communications (SATCC) systems allowing MQ-25 pilots access to existing ship radios. There are several ancillary systems that only have shore variants. The Shore Common Data Link
 (CDL) provides wideband line of sight command and control and data communications between the GCS and the MQ-25. The Ashore Routing Communication System (ARCS) is the interface between the
 Unmanned Carrier Aviation Transport System and the C4I wideband communication data networks. The URC-300 Voice Communication System is a set of radios for communication between the GCS and flight


LI 4269 - UMCS-Unman Carrier Aviation(UCA)Mission ...                                                   UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                    Volume 3 - 141
Navy                                                                                                           Page 1 of 19                                  P-1 Line #122
                                                                                               UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                        Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                      P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 03: Aviation Support Equipment / BSA 3:                                 4269 / UMCS-Unman Carrier Aviation(UCA)Mission Cntrl Stn
Aircraft Support Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                  Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 line personnel. UMCS leads the modification, engineering, and integration activities with NAVWAR C4I baseline systems. NAVWAR C4I is integrating the following baseline Program of Record systems to support
 MQ-25 operations: Consolidated Afloat Networks and Enterprise Services (CANES), Automated Digital Network System (ADNS), Shore Tactical Assured Command and Control (STACC), Network Tactical
 Common Data Link (NTCDL), CVN MUOS DMR (AN/SRC-61A(V)18 & AN/SRC-61A(V)19), Shore MUOS DMR (AN/FRC-147A(V)5), Classic Reach, Distributed Common Ground System-Navy (DCGS-N), Global
 Command and Control System-Maritime (GCCS-M), Automatic Identification System (AIS) & Naval Integrated Tactical Environment System-Next (NITES-Next).

 UMCS performs CVN infrastructure modifications and hardware installations on twelve (12) NIMITZ and FORD Class carriers and three (3) operational shore locations. Currently there are seven (7) workstations
 planned for each NIMITZ class carrier and nine (9) workstations per GCS planned for each FORD class carrier. The CONUS shore sites have two (2) GCSs each while the OCONUS site has one (1) GCS.
 There are nine (9) workstations planned for both the CONUS and OCONUS sites. All CVN Modification Planning and Integration activities follow the NAVSEA Navy Modernization Process (NMP) which identifies
 specific schedules for documentation, drawing, and hardware deliveries. CVN installations are conducted serially across multiple ship maintenance availability periods. The Navy Modernization Process can be
 divided into four phases. Phase 1 (three years prior to an availability period) is the CVN system planning and design phase that includes generation of technical data and top-level drawings as well as some long-
 lead procurement. Phase 2 (two years prior to an availability period) focuses on specific CVN hulls that includes ship checks, generation of drawings, and GCS/ancillary system procurement. Phase 3 (one-year
 prior to the availability period) is the final approval for design, drawings, schedule, and documentation. Phase 4 is the actual CVN Installation and Shipyard Services process that occurs during an availability
 period. A minimum of two six-month availability periods are required to complete modifications and installations. Phases 1, 2, and 3 repeat for each availability period. Therefore, it is possible that UMCS can be
 installing hardware and planning for future installation work on the same CVN or multiple CVNs in any given fiscal year. Modifications and installations are subject to change based on ship availability schedule
 updates.

 CVN Modification Planning and Integration requires extensive stakeholder coordination by a program to perform carrier modifications following NAVSEA processes and requirements for integration into a CVN
 Maintenance Availability (Avail) work package. Programs are responsible to prepare the technical documentation and detailed guidance information for each ship that will be modified per the Ship Change
 Documents so that Ship Installation Drawings (SIDs) can be produced. This process typically takes over a year, due to the complexity of modifications. The Unmanned Air Warfare Center (UAWC) Ship Change
 Documents are characterized by NAVSEA as complex since these impact several compartments, cooling, power and network/circuits on the ship. UMCS uses government and contractor resources to prepare
 the Ship Change Documents and supporting guidance packages across all hulls for both NIMITZ and FORD Class carriers. These resources also manage the communications and administrative paperwork
 levied on a Program by the Navy Modernization Process leading up to, during and after the ship installation. Guidance packages contain detailed design and interface specifications for the equipment, UAWC
 arrangement drawings for placement of hardware in the control room, schematics for cabling details, supporting details and analyses to address physical security, cyber design details, RF spectrum studies for
 topside antennas, and suitability provisions for the shipboard environment.

 CVN Installation and Shipyard Services involve the physical modifications to the ship. These services are the implementation of the CVN Modification Planning and Integration processes and are significant
 in scope and cost. The Planning Yard produces Ship Installation Drawings and a Shipyard approved work package with a detailed installation schedule. These installations are aligned with the Avail schedule
 to minimize impact to key events & milestones so that the ship exits the Avail on time. Contractor Alteration Installation Teams (AITs) perform the industrial work and hardware installation. AITs and their
 subcontractors must be approved by the Shipyard. Scheduling AITs are done well in advance of the Maintenance Avail, mostly due to the lengthy contracting process and for them to receive approved Ship
 Installation Drawings to plan the installation work, procure raw materials and align subcontractors. Following the approval of the Ship Change Documents and Ship Installation Drawings, industrial work can
 proceed during the aligned Avail. Industrial work includes setting up shipyard services (e.g. scaffolding, ventilation, air/electric for tools, storage containers on the pier, material handling/cranes), removal of
 old paint and hazardous coatings/materials, cutting/grinding of metal, welding structural members, foundations and fixtures, installation of electrical and network cabling, priming and painting, installation of
 thermal & acoustic insulation, fabrication and installation of ventilation ducting, modification/ increasing the size of cooling fans and coils, and the application of flooring. Once the industrial work has been
 completed, any hardware to be installed is brought aboard. If the hardware is of small enough size, it will be brought onboard the ship through the existing hatches. However, larger equipment, such as the fan
 room modifications, will need to have special holes cut in the deck to allow installation. The fully populated racks of the GCS require careful rigging to get the hardware through passageways and hatches into the
 space. Once these difficulties are overcome, hardware installation ensues, with cabling and other electrical/mechanical tasks going through various quality checks ultimately leading to a final System Operational
 Verification Test (SOVT).
